article_text:
Article 28
Regulation (EU) 2024/795 is amended as follows: (a)in Article 2, (1) point (a), subparagraph (iii) is replaced by the following: ‘(iii)biotechnologies, and any other technologies relevant for manufacturing of critical medicinal products as defined in Critical Medicines Act *; _________ *Regulation (EU) … of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down a framework for strengthening the availability and security of supply of critical medicinal products as well as for improving the availability of, and access to, medicinal products of common interest, and amending Regulation (EU) 2024/795.’ [D.G.: reference to be completed with the definitive title of the ‘Critical Medicines Act’ and with its publications references once they are available];’ (b)in Article 2, the following subparagraph is added in paragraph 3: ‘By way of derogation from the first subparagraph of this paragraph, the value chain for the development or manufacturing of medicinal products that fall within the scope of the [Critical Medicines Act] and that are referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii) of this Article, relates to finished dosage forms, as well as to active pharmaceutical ingredients and other key inputs necessary for the production of the finished dosage forms of critical medicinal products as defined in the Regulation.’; (c)in article 2, paragraph 8 is added: ‘8.Strategic projects designated in accordance with the [Critical Medicines Act] that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products shall be deemed to contribute to the STEP objective referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii).’; (d)in Article 4, paragraph 7 is replaced by the following: ‘7.Strategic projects recognised in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act [and the Critical Medicines Act] that fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation and that receive a contribution under the programmes referred to in Article 3 of this Regulation may also receive a contribution from any other Union programme, including funds under shared management, provided that those contributions do not cover the same costs. The rules of the relevant Union programme shall apply to the corresponding contribution to the strategic project. The cumulative funding shall not exceed the total eligible costs of the strategic project. The support from the different Union programmes may be calculated on a pro rata basis in accordance with the documents setting out the conditions for support.’; (e)in Article 6, paragraph 1, point c is replaced by the following: (c)details of projects that have been recognized as strategic projects under the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act and the [Critical Medicines Act], to the extent that they fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation. Chapter VIII Final provisions
provision_effect: Amends Regulation (EU) 2024/795 (STEP) to integrate critical medicinal products into its framework, allowing strategic projects under the Critical Medicines Act to qualify for STEP objectives and access cumulative EU funding.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for investment incentives and support for European manufacturing of critical medicines to strengthen supply chain security and competitiveness. The provision concretely acts in that direction by integrating the Critical Medicines Act into the STEP regulation, enabling strategic projects addressing supply chain vulnerabilities in critical medicinal products to qualify for EU funding and cumulative financing — directly supporting the investment and production capacity goals BPI advocates for.

source_text: The Act should avoid fragmentation and disruptive effects of uncoordinated or not-fit-for-purpose national policies and stockpiling requirements. Instead, geographically agnostic stocks would allow compliant allocation across MS in line with real-time patient needs where required stockpile levels would be calculated mindfully, based on forward looking demand forecast rather than on past data. The Act should imperatively provide for a coordination mechanism at EU level and adjusted, limited thresholds for specific products like PDMPs. The Act should aim to provide for a set of mandatory tender criteria and weight ranges to reward supply reliability. We remain concerned about the reference to localization of sourcing or manufacturing processes, as this could unintentionally lead to anticompetitive situations and the opposite effect to reward the stronger companies from a supply resilience perspective. Criteria must be mindfully defined to also look at shortage/supply history, safety and quality, agilities, redundancies, and other elements which are considered in shortage prevention plans. Collaborative (joint) procurement of EU MSs may be explored, provided this results in better demand predictability and tender management to reward resilience.
context_before: CSL Behring supports policies that enhance the EU open strategic autonomy and competitiveness, as key means to enhance supply security of PDMPs, without resorting to protectionism. It is clear that the EU has become increasingly dependent on a limited number of suppliers or manufactures of critical medicines, with many being located outside the EU. For PDMPs, the EU must recognize the upstream dependency on the availability of human plasma from the US. Hence, it must take urgent action to increase plasma collection within the EU and within an expanded network of trusted third countries, enhance demand planning and agilities in distribution operations. Furthermore, the Act should encourage Member States (MSs) to reconsider pricing and cost containment policies to improve the economic viability of critical medicines. The Act should strengthen the existing manufacturing base in Europe by supporting infrastructure development and modernization, know-how and skilled workforce, and qualified suppliers for critical manufacturing processes. It should also enable agile and flexible logistics: harmonized packaging, electronic information leaflets, and better coordinated and interconnected stockpiles across the EU. The Act should avoid fragmentation and disruptive effects of uncoordinated or not-fit-for-purpose national policies and stockpiling requirements
article_text:
Article 20
Safeguards related to Member States’ contingency stocks requirements and other security of supply measures Measures on security of supply applied in one Member State shall not result in any negative impact in other Member States. Member States shall, in particular, avoid such an impact when proposing and defining the scope and timing of any form of requirements for companies to hold contingency stocks. Member States shall ensure that any requirements they impose on companies in the supply chain to hold contingency stocks are proportionate and respect the principles of transparency and solidarity. Section II Collaborative procurements
provision_effect: Requires that Member States' security of supply measures, including contingency stock requirements, do not negatively impact other Member States, and must be proportionate, transparent, and solidarity-respecting.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CSL Behring explicitly advocates against fragmented, uncoordinated national stockpiling requirements and calls for proportionate, mindfully calculated thresholds with EU-level coordination and solidarity principles. Article 20 directly responds to this by requiring Member States to ensure their contingency stock requirements are proportionate, transparent, and do not negatively impact other Member States — concrete action in the same direction the organisation advocated for.

source_text: Furthermore, global solidarity and cooperation must be embedded in the CMA, aligning with the broader goal of Universal Health Coverage and ensuring equitable access to medicines worldwide. Addressing Disparities in Access to Critical Medicines The CMA must go beyond broad commitments and establish actionable mechanisms to address disparities in medicine access across Europe. Patients, regardless of where they live, should not face unnecessary delays or barriers to obtaining critical treatments. This requires coordinated stockpiling strategies, transparent procurement policies, and equitable pricing frameworks that guarantee fair and efficient distribution of medicines. Strengthening Supply Chain Resilience Without Undermining Solidarity Effective supply chain resilience requires strong coordination between authorities, institutions, and patient organisations. Engaging patient organisations is crucial to developing stockpiling and supply policies that address real-world needs.
context_before: Ref. Ares(2025)1550859 - 26/02/2025 Cancer Patients Europe (CPE) Response to the Consultation on the Critical Medicines Act Cancer Patients Europe (CPE) welcomes the opportunity to respond to the European Commission’s call for evidence on the Proposal for a Critical Medicines Act (CMA). We commend this initiative as a crucial step toward preventing and addressing shortages of essential medicines and ensuring timely and equal access for all patients across Europe. Addressing supply chain vulnerabilities is vital to guaranteeing the availability of life- saving treatments, particularly for cancer patients who depend on continuous and timely access to essential therapies. Ensuring a Public Health-Centered Approach While the CMA aims to strengthen pharmaceutical supply chains and reduce Europe’s external dependencies, it is essential that patient needs drive its implementation. Ensuring high-quality, safe, and effective medicines is critical for cancer patients, who rely on timely and continuous access to treatments that meet rigorous EU standards. Supply resilience should not come at the cost of quality or patient safety. Patients across Europe face significant disparities in medicine availability, both between and within Member States’ regions. To address this, the CMA must prioritise accessibility, affordability, safety, and transparency, ensuring a public health-centered approach that upholds quality and safety standards while effectively tackling shortages
context_after: Equally important is the meaningful participation of patient organisatons in both the design and implementation of these policies. As the primary recipients of medicines, patients must have a direct role in shaping policies that impact their access to treatment. Strengthening engagement with these stakeholders will help build trust, enhance transparency, and ensure that policies remain patient-centered and sustainable. The patient perspective is essential in ensuring that policies reflect the real needs and priorities of the wider population. Patients can provide valuable insights that help drive more inclusive and equitable policy decisions. Encouraging Innovation and Alternative Pathways for Drug Production The CMA should promote innovation and explore new pathways for medicines production, including repurposing off-patent medicines and supporting not-for-profit and public production facilities. Many shortages affect older, off-patent medicines that lack commercial appeal for industry suppliers. This includes essential antibiotics, where sustainable production is key to combating antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and preserving effective treatments for future generations. To address this, the EU and Member States should support public and not-for-profit initiatives focused on sustainable local production of critical medicines alongside commercial approaches to strengthen supply resilience and safeguard public health
article_text:
Article 19
Programmes supporting sustainability and resilience in public procurement procedures 1.By 6 months after entry into force of this Regulation each Member State shall establish a national programme supporting security of supply of critical medicinal products, including in public procurement procedures. Such programmes shall promote the consistent use of procurement requirements by contracting authorities within a given Member State as well as multi-winner approaches, where beneficial in light of the market analysis. Such programmes may also include measures for pricing and reimbursement supporting security of supply of those critical medicinal products that are not purchased through public procurement procedures. 2.Member States shall notify their programmes to the Commission in its role of the secretariat of the Critical Medicines Group. The Commission shall ensure the distribution to all members of the Critical Medicines Group forthwith. The Critical Medicines Group shall facilitate a discussion aiming to ensure coordination of national programmes including as regards the application of criteria mentioned in Article 18(2) and may issue opinions. Where the Critical Medicines Group issues an opinion concerning the national programmes, Member States shall give it due consideration and may take it into account when revising their programmes.

source_text: 4. EU Law Must Reflect Member States’ Human Rights Obligations International human rights law and standards require states to guarantee the right to sexual and reproductive health as an integral part of the right to the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health.16 International human rights bodies have affirmed that this entails core minimum obligations to guarantee access to essential drugs as defined by the World Health Organization and that non-compliance with these obligations cannot be justified under any circumstances.17 They have further held that restrictions on access to reproductive health goods that only women need must be removed.18 As such, the Critical Medicines Act is an opportunity for the EU to ensure compliance, across member states, with international human rights obligations. 5. Three Key Recommendations for a Comprehensive Critical Medicines Act Please find below three key recommendations to ensure the Critical Medicines Act is truly fit for purpose and comprehensive. The implementation of these recommendations will have an important impact on the health, lives and human rights of millions of patients in the EU: • The Critical Medicines Act should be comprehensive and inclusive of sexual and reproductive health medicines, including contraception and abortion medication, in line with the WHO List of Essential Medicines
context_before: Based on publicly available information, currently 12 EU member states have or are in the process of drafting their national list of essential medicines, based on the WHO list. However, there are significant disparities in availability and accessibility of these sexual and reproductive health medicines across the EU. The Union List of Critical Medicines is not comprehensive as it only contains Mifepristone. It is imperative that the Critical Medicines Act go beyond the framework of the Union List of Critical Medicines, and that the List be revised and brought into line with the WHO List. As such, contraception and abortion medication must be included in the Critical Medicines Act as critical medicines, given the limited and lack of alternatives. Additionally, given the time- sensitive nature of these medicines, insufficient supply results in serious and irreparable harm to patients, warranting EU-level safeguards to support the security of supply and the availability of these critical medicines. 4
context_after: • The Critical Medicines Act should be more inclusive than the Union List of Critical Medicines, which should be revised to ensure it is brought in line with the WHO List of Essential Medicines. • The Critical Medicines Act should ensure any monitoring of the availability of critical medicines throughout the EU is gender sensitive. 4
article_text:
Article 1
Objectives and subject matter 1.The objective of this Regulation is to strengthen the security of supply and the availability of critical medicinal products within the Union, thereby ensuring a high level of public health protection and supporting the security of the Union. The objective of this Regulation is also to improve the availability and accessibility of other medicinal products, where the functioning of the market does not otherwise sufficiently ensure the availability and accessibility of those medicinal products to patients, whilst giving due consideration to the appropriateness to ensure the affordability of medicinal products. 2.To achieve the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Regulation sets out a framework to: (a)facilitate investments in manufacturing capacity for critical medicinal products, their active substances and other key inputs in the Union; (b)lower the risk of supply disruptions and strengthen availability by incentivising supply chain diversification and resilience in the public procurement procedures of critical medicinal products and other medicinal products of common interest; (c)leverage the aggregated demand of participating Member States through collaborative procurement procedures, and (d)support the diversification of supply chains also by facilitating the conclusion of strategic partnerships.
provision_effect: Establishes the objectives and framework of the Critical Medicines Regulation, focusing on supply security, availability, affordability, manufacturing investment, supply chain resilience, collaborative procurement, and strategic partnerships for critical and other medicinal products.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation specifically advocates that the Critical Medicines Act should include sexual and reproductive health medicines (contraception, abortion medication) in line with the WHO Essential Medicines List. The provision establishes the regulation's general objectives and framework for critical medicinal products but does not specify which medicines are included or excluded — it neither incorporates nor explicitly excludes reproductive health medicines. There is a real connection (the org is commenting directly on this regulation's scope), but it is genuinely unclear from this provision alone whether the org's specific ask is satisfied or ignored.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2025)1575249 - 27/02/2025 Feedback regarding the Critical Medicines Act February 2025 The Center for Reproductive Rights welcomes the proposal for EU legislation on critical medicines and other medicinal products of common interests, which aims to ensure equal access to essential medicines and address shortages of supply. The initiative offers an important opportunity to ensure EU law effectively addresses shortages of and accessibility barriers for essential sexual and reproductive health medicines across EU member states and aligns EU preventative measures with international public health guidelines. Ensuring the upcoming Critical Medicines Act is fit for purpose is also critical in light of the disruption in access to contraception and abortion medication during the Covid-19 pandemic.1 The inclusion of contraception and abortion medication within the scope of the upcoming EU Critical Medicines Act is essential for safeguarding public health, ensuring equitable access to sexual and reproductive healthcare, and upholding fundamental human rights. These medicines are critical medicines. Lack of access to these medicines, which serve to address serious obstetric and gynaecological conditions and emergencies, results in serious harm including irreversible consequences for patients, and poses important challenges to health systems
context_after: Yet, shortages of sexual and reproductive health medicines are “a longstanding and well documented crisis”2 with very frequent interruptions in their production and supply, including complete shortages. Including contraception and abortion medication within the scope of the EU Critical Medicines Act will contribute to the EU meeting key targets under the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly ensuring universal access to sexual and reproductive health-care services, including for family planning and ensuring access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines for all. The European Parliament has also clearly called for universal access to a range of high-quality and accessible modern contraceptive methods and supplies, including emergency contraception, and abortion medication in the EU.3 1. Contraception and Abortion Medication are Critical Medicines • Contraception Access to safe and effective contraception is a fundamental aspect of sexual and reproductive health care. Several forms of contraception are on the World Health Organization (WHO) List of Essential Medicines and are necessary for the treatment of a wide number of obstetric and gynaecological conditions.4 Access to contraception is key to prevent unintended pregnancies, which can have significant physical, emotional, and financial implications for individuals and families, and cause particularly significant harms to minors in cases of adolescent pregnancies. article_text:
Article 22
Commission procurement on behalf of or in the name of Member States 1.By way of derogation from Article 168(3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509 where nine or more Member States jointly request the Commission to procure on their behalf, or in their name, the Commission may initiate a procurement procedure under the conditions set out in this Article when the procurement relates to medicinal products belonging to one of the following categories below; (a)critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability evaluation has identified a vulnerability in the supply chains or for which the MSSG has recommended a common procurement initiative; (b)medicinal products of common interest, for which a joint clinical assessment report has been published pursuant to Article 12(4) Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 of the European Parliament and the Council , or which have undergone a clinical assessment carried out under the voluntary cooperation among Member States as per Article 23(1) point (e) of that Regulation. 2.The joint request referred to in paragraph 1 shall only be made where the medicinal product concerned fulfils one of the criteria set out in that paragraph and if the requested procurement procedure will help to improve the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the Union or ensure the availability and accessibility of medicinal products of common interest, as applicable. 3.The participation in the procurement procedure shall be open to all Member States. The Commission shall inform all Member States of the request, through the Critical Medicines Group, and invite them to join the procedure. 4.The Commission shall assess the utility, necessity and proportionality of the request and whether the request is justified in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall in particular verify whether the procurement could constitute discrimination or restriction to trade or a distortion to competition. 5.The Commission shall inform the interested Member States within one month of the request of its decision and state its reasons in case of a refusal. 6.If in light of the Commission assessment, it is necessary, in order to achieve the objectives of this Regulation, to conduct the procurement as exclusive for the Member States or to agree to minimum binding quantities, the Commission agreement to pursue the procedure may be conditioned upon acceptance of these conditions by interested Member States. 7.Except for the derogations provided for in this Regulation, the procurement referred to in this Article shall be carried out in accordance with Article 168 (3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to conduct joint procurement of critical medicinal products or medicinal products of common interest on behalf of nine or more Member States, subject to specific eligibility criteria and procedural conditions.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's position focuses specifically on ensuring that contraception and abortion medication are included within the scope of the Critical Medicines Act, not on the joint procurement mechanism itself. The org text contains no specific advocacy stance on Commission-led joint procurement procedures, their conditions, or their design — making it substantively unrelated to what Article 22 establishes.

article_text:
Article 25
Establishment of Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.A Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘Critical Medicines Group’) is hereby established. 2.The Member States and the Commission are Members of the Critical Medicines Group. Each Member State shall appoint a maximum of two high-level permanent representatives, with the expertise relevant for implementing all the different measures set out in this Regulation. Where relevant as regards the function and expertise, Member States may appoint different representatives in relation to different tasks of the Critical Medicines Group. Appointed permanent representatives shall ensure the necessary coordination within their respective Member State. The Agency shall have an observer status. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall work closely with the MSSG, the Agency, and national authorities responsible for medicinal products. For discussions where input from the medicines regulatory authorities’ perspective is necessary, the Critical Medicines Group may organise joint meetings with the MSSG. 4.The Commission shall organise and coordinate the work of the Critical Medicines Group by means of the Secretariat. 5.A representative of the Commission shall chair the meetings of the Critical Medicines Group. 6.The Critical Medicines Group, at the proposal of the Chair or any its members, may decide to establish a working group. 7.The Critical Medicines Group shall use its best endeavours to reach consensus, where possible. Members with diverging positions may request that their positions and the grounds on which they are based be recorded in the Critical Medicines Group’s position.
provision_effect: Establishes the Critical Medicines Coordination Group, defining its membership, governance structure, working procedures, and coordination mechanisms between Member States and the Commission.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's advocacy position focuses on ensuring sexual and reproductive health medicines are included in the scope of the Critical Medicines Act, framed through a human rights lens. Article 25 purely establishes the governance and structural setup of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group — it takes no substantive position on which medicines are covered or prioritised, and does not address reproductive health or human rights obligations in any way. There is no meaningful connection between the org's specific ask and this procedural/structural provision.

article_text:
Article 26
Tasks of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.The Critical Medicines Group shall facilitate coordination in the implementation of this Regulation and, where appropriate, advise the Commission, so as to maximise the impact of the measures envisaged and to avoid any unintended effects on the internal market. 2.In order to attain the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Critical Medicines Group shall perform the following tasks: (a)facilitate coordination on strategic orientation of the financial support for strategic projects, including by exchanging information on the manufacturing capacity for a given critical medicinal product, existing or planned, in the Member States and facilitate discussion on the capacity needed in the Union to strengthen its supply security and availability of critical medicinal products within the Union; (b)facilitate exchanges on the national programmes referred to in Article 19 and enable cooperation on and coordination of Member States public procurement policies with regard to critical medicinal products; (c)facilitate discussion of the need for a collaborative procurement initiative for a given medicinal product; (d)advise the MSSG to provide the order of priority of critical medicinal products for vulnerability evaluation, and propose a review or an update of existing evaluations where necessary. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall enable the exchanges of information between the Member States and the Commission as referred to in Article 17 and shall enable, where necessary, a coordination of respective actions aiming to attain the objectives of this Regulation. 4.The Critical Medicines Group shall periodically discuss the potential contribution of strategic partnerships to the objectives of this Regulation, prioritisation of third countries for this purpose, and the consistency and potential synergies between Member States’ cooperation with relevant third countries and the actions carried out by the Union. 5.The Critical Medicines Group, at the Commission’s request, may provide an opinion on matters related to the application of this Regulation in the context of performing tasks as referred to in this Article. Chapter VI International cooperation
provision_effect: Establishes the tasks of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group, including facilitating coordination on strategic orientation, financial support, procurement policies, and vulnerability evaluations for critical medicinal products.

context_before: Deployment of innovative blood safety technologies, such as pathogen inactivation, could increase EU supply sufficiency and resilience. EU Blood Safety Cerus advocates for a clear regulatory environment for blood and blood products. We support innovative methods and technologies that help foster high safety standards for the EU health ecosystem and its patients. Against this backdrop, we welcome the Substances of Human Origin (SoHO) Regulation, published in June 2024, and its provisions requiring blood banks to ensure that the blood transmitted to recipients is safe including through measures that reduce or eliminate any potential pathogens or microbial contamination. We acknowledge the Regulation’s emphasis on critical SoHO supply sufficiency (Article 62), national SoHO emergency plans (Article 63), and supply alerts for critical SoHO (Article 64), which are crucial for maintaining a reliable and responsive blood supply. While the SoHO Regulation addresses safety and quality, however, it does not fully address the critical issue of blood supply sufficiency and its foundational role in health system resilience, particularly in the face of emerging threats and disruptions. This gap highlights the urgent need to include blood, blood components, and critical medical devices in the scope of the proposed Critical Medicines Act. context_after: a risk of transfusion transmitted infections which could lead to a collapse of blood supplies during a pandemic. Moreover, climate adverse events can also disrupt EU blood supplies. In this regard, and in light of the increase of tropical diseases in Europe, the European Environmental Agency underscores the need to safeguard the blood supply with pathogen inactivation being one of the strategies capable to “prevent cascading risk pathways affecting the blood supply.” Blood transfusions are often needed in emergencies, surgeries, and medical conditions requiring blood replacement. The Commission should follow the approach of the World Health Organization (WHO) who includes human blood and blood products in its Essential Medicines List (EML). Blood is just like other critical medicines, products that satisfy the health-care needs of the majority of the population. This according to the WHO means it should be available at all times, in adequate amounts and in appropriate dosage forms, with assured quality and affordability. While the recently adopted SoHO Regulation, repealing the Blood Directive (2002), provides harmonized measures to ensure the highest safety and quality of blood available, it does not sufficiently address blood supply sufficiency and its foundational role in health system resiliency. source_text: It is therefore necessary to seek and support solutions that help to use surplus medicines in one country in another country where there is a shortage. From this perspective, the pharmaceutical sector and its supply chain (including manufacturers, MAH, direct and parallel distributors) has therefore already been playing a key role in mitigating shortages and, as explained in the section below, we believe this should be further considered in the context of this work. Assessing the vulnerabilities in the industrial supply chains of Critical Medicines • The creation of a list of Critical Vulnerable Medicines is a valuable tool for identifying where continued supply is considered a priority for the EU. However, it is essential that the methodology for compiling this list includes a thorough assessment of available alternatives. The current EU Critical Medicines list, firstly released in December 2023, is compiled based on medical criteria including the severity of the condition, availability of alternatives and additional input from Member States, and it is kept updated on a regular basis. Justification: These criteria, particularly the availability of alternatives, should also be considered when establishing a European list of Critical Vulnerable Medicines
context_before: Ref. Ares(2025)1583364 - 27/02/2025 Critical Medicines Act - feedback from the Czech Chamber of Commerce The Czech Chamber of Commerce welcomes the opportunity to comment on the call for evidence regarding Critical Medicines Act. Below you will find comments from our members. General comments: We greatly appreciate the report and the efforts of the Critical Medicines Alliance in addressing the challenges contributing to shortages of Critical Medicines and in proposing solutions to strengthen supply chain resilience. Medicine shortages have increased significantly in recent years, and all pharmaceutical industry has been impacted directly (e.g. independent distributors, which rely on surplus supplies after national markets). Regarding the initiative's scope, we would like to note that, as reported by EFPIA1, in-patent medicines account for only 4% of reported shortages, indicating that most shortages affect generic medicines. Furthermore, 90% of reported shortages of Critical Medicines pertain to generics2, as highlighted in the report. Therefore, we believe the report's focus and the proposed solutions should primarily target this area of medicines. It’s important to note that most shortages are not pan-European but occur at national, regional, or local levels and therefore the situation can be addressed using existing resources and without significant regulatory intervention
context_after: Justification: These criteria, particularly the availability of alternatives, should also be considered when establishing a European list of Critical Vulnerable Medicines. Strengthening European manufacturing of critical medicines by investing in EU strategic projects • We advocate for a stronger emphasis on EU-level investments over national incentives to address the structural issues underlying medicine shortages. A coordinated, European approach will better preserve the integrity of the internal market and foster 1 EFPIA, Medicine Shortages: EFPIA Proposal for Action, 2024: https://www.efpia.eu/media/adnjlfjd/medicine-shortages-efpia-proposal-for- action.pdf 2 According to IQVIA, 8.2 billion packs of generic medicines are sold in the EU every year. In value this is about € 30 billion of yearly sales. According to IQVIA, out of the 8.2 billion packs sold in Europe, approximate 8 million are parallel traded. That is less than 0.1% of all packs sold in the EU. These parallel traded packs have a value of about € 180 million, thus accounting for around 0.6% of the generic market in value. article_text:
Article 3
Definitions For the purpose of this Regulation, the following definitions shall apply: (1) ‘medicinal product’ means a medicinal product as defined in Article 4 point (1) of Directive (EU) …/… of the European Parliament and of the Council [reference to be added to corresponding Article after adoption of cf. COM(2023) 192 final]; (2) ‘key input’ means input material other than an active substance required in the manufacturing process of a given medicinal product, including primary packaging materials, excipients, solvents and reagents; (3) ‘active substance’ means an active substance as defined in Article 4 point (3) of Directive (EU) …/… [reference to be added to corresponding Article after adoption of cf. COM(2023) 192 final]; (4) ‘critical medicinal product’ means a medicinal product for which insufficient supply results in serious harm or risk of serious harm to patients as defined in Article 4 point (13) of Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]; (5) ‘medicinal product of common interest’ means a medicinal product, other than a critical medicinal product, for which in three or more Member States the functioning of the market does not sufficiently ensure the availability and accessibility to patients in the quantities and presentations necessary to cover the needs of patients in those Member States; (6) ‘vulnerability in the supply chains’ means risks and weaknesses within the supply chains of critical medicinal products, identified at the aggregated level, taking into account all authorised medicinal products in the EU and grouped under a common name with the same route of administration and formulation, that compromise the continuous supply of such medicinal products to patients in the Union; (7) ‘vulnerability evaluation’ means the evaluation of the supply chains of critical medicinal products to identify their vulnerabilities performed by the MSSG in accordance with Regulation (EU) …/… of the European Parliament and of the Council [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]; (8) ‘common name’ means a common name as defined in Article 4 point (48) of Directive (EU) …/… of the European Parliament and of the Council [reference to be added to corresponding Article after adoption of cf. COM(2023) 192 final]; (9) ‘contracting authorities’ means contracting authorities as defined in Article 2(1) point (1) of Directive 2014/24/EU; (10) ‘strategic project’ means an industrial project identified pursuant to the criteria set out in Article 5; (11) ‘project promoter’ means any undertaking or consortium of undertakings developing a strategic project; (12) ‘permit granting process’ means a process covering all relevant permits to build and operate a strategic project, including building, chemical and grid connection permits and environmental assessments and authorisations where those are required and encompassing all applications and procedures; (13) ‘innovative manufacturing process’ means a novel manufacturing process and technology or novel application of an existing technology, including, but not limited to, decentralised manufacturing, continuous manufacturing, Artificial Intelligence, platform techniques, 3D manufacturing; (15) ‘Member States’ cross-border procurement’ means a procurement procedure initiated between the contracting authorities from different Member States on the basis of Article 39 of Directive 2014/24/EC; (16) ‘procurement on behalf of or in the name of the Member States’ means a procurement procedure initiated at the request of Member States and mandating the Commission to act as a central purchasing body on behalf of, or in the name of, the requesting Member States, as provided for in Article 168(3) of Regulation (EU) 2024/2509; (17) ‘joint procurement’ means a procurement procedure carried out jointly by the Commission and Member States, as provided for in Article 168(2) of Regulation (EU) 2024/2509; (18) ‘supplier’ means the manufacturer or marketing authorisation holder of finished dosage forms, or manufacturer of key inputs or active substances; (19) ‘strategic partnership’ means a commitment between the Union and a third country, group of third countries or international organisations to increase cooperation related to one or more critical medicinal products that is established through a non-binding instrument and which facilitates beneficial outcomes for both the Union and the relevant third country, group of third countries or international organisation. Chapter II Strengthening the Union’s security of supply
provision_effect: Defines key terms used throughout the Regulation, including 'critical medicinal product', 'vulnerability in the supply chains', and 'vulnerability evaluation', without establishing any substantive requirements or actions.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org specifically advocates that the methodology for identifying Critical Vulnerable Medicines must include a thorough assessment of available alternatives. The provision defines 'vulnerability in the supply chains' and 'vulnerability evaluation', which are directly related to this subject, but as purely definitional text it does not specify the methodology or criteria to be used — making it genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's specific ask about including availability of alternatives in the assessment methodology.

Ares(2025)1583364 - 27/02/2025 Critical Medicines Act - feedback from the Czech Chamber of Commerce The Czech Chamber of Commerce welcomes the opportunity to comment on the call for evidence regarding Critical Medicines Act. Below you will find comments from our members. General comments: We greatly appreciate the report and the efforts of the Critical Medicines Alliance in addressing the challenges contributing to shortages of Critical Medicines and in proposing solutions to strengthen supply chain resilience. Medicine shortages have increased significantly in recent years, and all pharmaceutical industry has been impacted directly (e.g. independent distributors, which rely on surplus supplies after national markets). Regarding the initiative's scope, we would like to note that, as reported by EFPIA1, in-patent medicines account for only 4% of reported shortages, indicating that most shortages affect generic medicines. Furthermore, 90% of reported shortages of Critical Medicines pertain to generics2, as highlighted in the report. Therefore, we believe the report's focus and the proposed solutions should primarily target this area of medicines. It’s important to note that most shortages are not pan-European but occur at national, regional, or local levels and therefore the situation can be addressed using existing resources and without significant regulatory intervention

article_text:
Article 15
Financial support by Member States 1.Without prejudice to Articles 107 and 108 TFEU, Member States may prioritise financial support to strategic projects that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products identified following a vulnerability evaluation and with due consideration to the strategic orientations of the Critical Medicines Group referred to in Article 26(2) point (a). 2.For as long as the critical medicinal product is on the Union List of Critical Medicinal Products, an undertaking that has benefitted from financial support for a strategic project shall prioritise supply to the Union market and use its very best efforts to ensure that the critical medicinal product remains available in the Member States where it is being marketed. 3.The Member State that provided financial support to a strategic project may request such undertaking to provide the necessary supplies of a critical medicinal product, active substance or key inputs, as applicable, to the Union market to avoid shortages in one or several Member States. Any Member State that encounters a threat of shortages of the critical medicinal product in question may demand the Member State that provided financial support to submit a request on its behalf.
provision_effect: Allows Member States to provide financial support to strategic projects addressing supply chain vulnerabilities for critical medicinal products, and requires beneficiaries to prioritise supply to the Union market and respond to shortage requests.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org advocates for robust methodology in compiling the Critical Medicines list (including assessment of alternatives) and for cross-border redistribution of surplus medicines, which touches on the same supply chain vulnerability framework underpinning Article 15. However, the provision specifically concerns Member State financial support mechanisms and supply obligations for strategic project beneficiaries — neither directly delivering nor contradicting the org's specific asks about list methodology or surplus redistribution. A real thematic connection exists, but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply bypasses the org's stated positions.

article_text:
Article 19
Programmes supporting sustainability and resilience in public procurement procedures 1.By 6 months after entry into force of this Regulation each Member State shall establish a national programme supporting security of supply of critical medicinal products, including in public procurement procedures. Such programmes shall promote the consistent use of procurement requirements by contracting authorities within a given Member State as well as multi-winner approaches, where beneficial in light of the market analysis. Such programmes may also include measures for pricing and reimbursement supporting security of supply of those critical medicinal products that are not purchased through public procurement procedures. 2.Member States shall notify their programmes to the Commission in its role of the secretariat of the Critical Medicines Group. The Commission shall ensure the distribution to all members of the Critical Medicines Group forthwith. The Critical Medicines Group shall facilitate a discussion aiming to ensure coordination of national programmes including as regards the application of criteria mentioned in Article 18(2) and may issue opinions. Where the Critical Medicines Group issues an opinion concerning the national programmes, Member States shall give it due consideration and may take it into account when revising their programmes.
provision_effect: Requires each Member State to establish a national programme supporting security of supply of critical medicinal products, including in public procurement procedures, promoting consistent use of procurement requirements and multi-winner approaches, with coordination facilitated by the Critical Medicines Group.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org supports systematic application of specific MEAT criteria in Critical Medicines public procurement and endorses EU-level coordination, which aligns directionally with the provision's establishment of national procurement programmes. However, the org also raises concerns about national stockpiling requirements and disruption to pre-existing contracts — concerns that could be either addressed or exacerbated by this provision — making it genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies or contradicts the org's overall position.

article_text:
Article 20
Safeguards related to Member States’ contingency stocks requirements and other security of supply measures Measures on security of supply applied in one Member State shall not result in any negative impact in other Member States. Member States shall, in particular, avoid such an impact when proposing and defining the scope and timing of any form of requirements for companies to hold contingency stocks. Member States shall ensure that any requirements they impose on companies in the supply chain to hold contingency stocks are proportionate and respect the principles of transparency and solidarity. Section II Collaborative procurements
provision_effect: Requires Member States to ensure that contingency stock requirements imposed on companies are proportionate, transparent, and do not negatively impact other Member States, while promoting solidarity.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The Czech Chamber of Commerce explicitly advocates for discouraging Member State-level contingency stocks altogether, preferring EU-level stocks instead, and warns against overlapping requirements. The provision does not discourage national contingency stocks but rather regulates them with proportionality, transparency, and solidarity safeguards. While the provision's proportionality and non-duplication principles partially echo the org's concern about overlapping requirements, the org's core ask — that national contingency stocks be discouraged or replaced by EU-level stocks — is neither delivered nor explicitly contradicted by this provision, making the direction genuinely ambiguous.

article_text:
Article 24
Agreement concerning procedures under Articles 22 and 23 1.Member States participating in the procurement procedures covered by Articles 22 and 23 shall share with the Commission any information relevant for the procurement procedure. Member States shall provide resources necessary for the successful conclusion of the procedure, in particular through involvement of staff with expertise and knowledge. 2.An agreement between the Member States and the Commission shall determine the practical arrangements governing the procurement procedure, liabilities to be assumed and the decision-making process. Chapter V Critical Medicines Coordination Group
provision_effect: Establishes that Member States participating in joint procurement must share relevant information with the Commission and provide necessary resources, with a formal agreement governing practical arrangements and liabilities.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org raises a concern about fair access to procurement-relevant data for all suppliers (including parallel distributors), which touches on the information-sharing dimension of procurement procedures. However, Article 24 governs information sharing between Member States and the Commission — not between procuring authorities and suppliers — so it is genuinely unclear whether this provision addresses or ignores the org's specific concern about supplier access to data in procurement processes.

context_before: At the same time, the Marketing Authorisation Holder (MAH) plays a crucial role in the supply of medicines to individual EU member states. Only the MAH has the detailed knowledge and awareness of the intervals and volumes in which medicinal products are manufactured for specific markets. Based on this information, the MAH is able to provide local government authorities with the information necessary to assess whether there is a risk of unavailability of the medicinal product on the market in a given situation. Based on this assessment, the regulatory authority may apply measures to restrict parallel exports if necessary. We believe that the Critical Medicines Alliance should, in seeking a solution to the problem, take into account all of these possibilities and benefits. article_text:
Article 29
Obligation of the market actors to provide information 1.Marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators in the supply and distribution chains of critical medicinal products including their key inputs and active substances or medicinal products of common interest shall upon request provide the Commission or national authorities, as relevant, the requested information necessary for the purpose of application of this Regulation. 2.The Commission and national authorities of the Member States shall aim to avoid duplication of the information requested and submitted. 3.The Commission and national authorities of the Member States shall assess the merits of duly substantiated confidentiality claims made by marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators, requested to provide information per paragraph 1, and shall protect any information that is commercially confidential against unjustified disclosure.
provision_effect: Requires marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators to provide information to the Commission or national authorities upon request, while protecting commercially confidential information against unjustified disclosure.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org raises a specific concern about information asymmetry in procurement processes — namely that non-commercially sensitive information held by Marketing Authorisation Holders should be accessible to other tender participants (e.g., parallel distributors). The provision deals with information obligations of market actors toward authorities, not toward other market participants in procurement contexts. There is a tangential connection (both concern information held by MAHs and economic operators), but the provision addresses a different direction of information flow (to authorities, not to competitors/other tenderers), making it genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's specific concern.

source_text: Ensuring a secure supply for sexual and reproductive and maternal health medicines ● Many sexual and reproductive health (SRH) and maternal health medicines are essential for public health, yet they often face severe shortages, both in Europe and globally. ● In line with the WHO List of Essential Medicines, the Critical Medicines Act should ensure that contraceptives, maternal health medications, and treatments for gender- specific diseases are included in the Union List of Critical Medicines. These medicines have a fundamental role in sexual and reproductive health care as they address serious obstetric and gynaecological conditions and emergencies. Lack of timely access may result in serious harm, irreversible consequences for patients and serious challenges for health systems, further exacerbating health inequalities, creating serious risks and leading to severe impacts on the lives, health and well-being of women and girls. ● The EU should prioritize the availability of SRH medicines within the EU but also in partner countries and regions (e.g., Africa) in its procurement policies and support gender-sensitive R&D and medicine accessibility strategies. ● The Act should also include monitoring mechanisms to track shortages of SRH medicines across EU member states and ensure timely interventions to prevent supply disruptions, particularly in times of crisis, as seen during the COVID-19 pandemic
context_before: ● The EU-Africa Global Health Partnership and the Team Europe Initiative (TEI) on Manufacturing and Access to Vaccines, Medicines, and Health Technologies (MAV+) should be leveraged to support joint manufacturing capabilities, regulatory harmonization, and pandemic preparedness. Synergies should also be built between the TEI on Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) and MAV+, to support manufacturing and effective supplies of sexual and reproductive health (SRH) tools, including contraceptives ● Support for regional health initiatives such as the Africa CDC’s Partnership for African Health Products Manufacturing (PHAHM) should be integrated into the Act to build long-term sustainability. ● The Act should be fully in line with the goals and ambitions of the EU Global Health Strategy, ensuring a strong EU’s role in promoting global health security. Recommendation: The Critical Medicines Act should explicitly recognize the EU’s role in strengthening global health security, particularly through investments in research and pharmaceutical production in partner countries.
context_after: Recommendation: The Act should adopt a gender-sensitive approach and include sexual and reproductive health (SRH) medicines and maternal health drugs in the list of critical medicines. Strengthening EU Support for Global Health R&D and Neglected Diseases ● Many neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), tuberculosis, and malaria disproportionately affect Low and Middle Income Countries (LMICs), yet they receive limited commercial R&D investment. Some of these diseases are already present or may be spreading to the EU due to climate change. ● The EU should integrate financial and regulatory incentives for the development, manufacturing and distribution of essential medicines for neglected diseases, including through public-private partnerships and funding via Horizon Europe. ● The Critical Medicines Act should include mechanisms to support the development, manufacturing and distribution of non-commercially attractive medicines, ensuring equitable medicine access for all. ● The Human Development Accelerator (HDX) partnership, co-financed by the EU, European Investment Bank, and the Gates Foundation, should be leveraged to facilitate blended finance mechanisms that encourage global health innovation.
article_text:
Article 1
Objectives and subject matter 1.The objective of this Regulation is to strengthen the security of supply and the availability of critical medicinal products within the Union, thereby ensuring a high level of public health protection and supporting the security of the Union. The objective of this Regulation is also to improve the availability and accessibility of other medicinal products, where the functioning of the market does not otherwise sufficiently ensure the availability and accessibility of those medicinal products to patients, whilst giving due consideration to the appropriateness to ensure the affordability of medicinal products. 2.To achieve the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Regulation sets out a framework to: (a)facilitate investments in manufacturing capacity for critical medicinal products, their active substances and other key inputs in the Union; (b)lower the risk of supply disruptions and strengthen availability by incentivising supply chain diversification and resilience in the public procurement procedures of critical medicinal products and other medicinal products of common interest; (c)leverage the aggregated demand of participating Member States through collaborative procurement procedures, and (d)support the diversification of supply chains also by facilitating the conclusion of strategic partnerships.
provision_effect: Establishes the objectives and framework of the Critical Medicines Act, including strengthening supply security, improving availability/accessibility of critical and other medicinal products, facilitating manufacturing investments, incentivising supply chain diversification, and enabling collaborative procurement.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: DSW specifically advocates for SRH and maternal health medicines to be included in the Critical Medicines Act's scope, for supply security measures, monitoring mechanisms, and procurement policies prioritising availability — all of which align with the concrete objectives and framework established in Article 1, including supply chain resilience, collaborative procurement, and ensuring availability and accessibility of critical medicinal products. The provision takes action in the same direction DSW advocates for, even if it does not explicitly name SRH medicines.

source_text: ● While the Act may not cover some of these areas (e.g., R&D), consistency and synergies should be sought with other complementary EU legislation, such as the EU general pharmaceutical legislation currently under discussion. Recommendation: The Act should commit to long-term EU investment in medicines for neglected diseases, ensuring that EU-supported research benefits low-resource settings as well as EU citizens. Sustainable and Equitable Procurement Models ● EU procurement models should include criteria beyond cost, incorporating supply chain resilience, sustainability, and global health impact. ● Incentives should be introduced for local pharmaceutical manufacturing in LMICs, particularly in Africa, through investment de-risking mechanisms such as those promoted under MAV+ and EFSD+ guarantees. ● The EU should facilitate technology transfer and capacity building in partner countries to ensure a sustainable supply of critical medicines. ● The Act should also ensure that procurement policies promote equitable access to SRH medicines, including contraceptives and abortion medication, across all EU member states. Ensuring these medicines remain constantly available is crucial to upholding public health and human rights. Recommendation: The EU should introduce sustainable and equitable procurement strategies that support ethical supply chains and international cooperation in medicine production

Strengthening EU Support for Global Health R&D and Neglected Diseases ● Many neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), tuberculosis, and malaria disproportionately affect Low and Middle Income Countries (LMICs), yet they receive limited commercial R&D investment. Some of these diseases are already present or may be spreading to the EU due to climate change. ● The EU should integrate financial and regulatory incentives for the development, manufacturing and distribution of essential medicines for neglected diseases, including through public-private partnerships and funding via Horizon Europe. ● The Critical Medicines Act should include mechanisms to support the development, manufacturing and distribution of non-commercially attractive medicines, ensuring equitable medicine access for all. ● The Human Development Accelerator (HDX) partnership, co-financed by the EU, European Investment Bank, and the Gates Foundation, should be leveraged to facilitate blended finance mechanisms that encourage global health innovation.
context_after: Recommendation: The EU should introduce sustainable and equitable procurement strategies that support ethical supply chains and international cooperation in medicine production. Enhancing Transparency and Civil Society Participation in Governance ● The Act should establish a formal advisory mechanism to include civil society, patient organizations, and global health experts in the governance of critical medicine policies. ● Transparent monitoring and public reporting should be required to assess medicine shortages and their impact on vulnerable populations, including in LMICs. ● A structured dialogue with international and regional health organizations (e.g., WHO, Africa CDC) should be included to align EU measures with global health priorities. ● A dedicated EU mechanism for monitoring the availability and accessibility of essential SRH medicines should be established to prevent and respond to future shortages. The Act must ensure proactive measures are in place to avoid disruptions in access to these critical medicines. Recommendation: The governance of the Critical Medicines Act should ensure civil society organizations (CSOs) and global health stakeholders are actively involved in decision-making and monitoring processes.
article_text:
Article 16
Financial support from the Union 1.For the duration of the Multiannual Financial Framework 2021-2027 strategic projects may be supported by Union funding, including but not limited to such Union programmes as the EU4Health Programme , Horizon Europe , and the Digital Europe Programme provided that such support is in line with the objectives set out in the regulations establishing those programmes. 2.At the request of a project promoter, justified by necessity to provide results of vulnerability evaluation for the purpose of an application for Union funding, the designated authority shall assess whether a strategic project addresses a vulnerability in the supply chains identified following the vulnerability evaluation. The designated authority shall provide its assessment to a project promoter within 15 working days of its request. The designated authority shall inform the Commission about the strategic projects identified as addressing an existing vulnerability in the supply chains without delay.
provision_effect: Establishes that strategic projects may receive Union funding from programmes such as EU4Health, Horizon Europe, and Digital Europe, and sets out a process for designated authorities to assess whether strategic projects address supply chain vulnerabilities for funding application purposes.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org advocates for long-term EU investment in medicines (including for neglected diseases and LMICs) and for procurement/funding models that incorporate supply chain resilience, sustainability, and global health impact. The provision does establish a Union funding support mechanism for strategic projects tied to supply chain vulnerability assessments, which partially overlaps with the org's call for EU investment and supply chain resilience. However, the provision is silent on the specific dimensions the org prioritises — equitable access, neglected diseases, LMIC manufacturing, technology transfer, or SRH medicines — making it genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the org's specific advocacy positions.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2025)1585600 - 27/02/2025 DSW submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Critical Medicines Act The Critical Medicines Act presents an important opportunity to enhance the resilience of Europe’s pharmaceutical supply chains. However, to ensure a truly effective Critical Medicines Act, we urge the Commission to adopt a global, inclusive, and forward-looking approach that reflects the EU’s responsibility in global health security. Integrating global health R&D, gender-sensitive policies, and sustainable procurement models will ensure that the Act not only safeguards EU medicine security but also contributes to a healthier and more equitable world. At the same time, this will help to further strengthen health security at EU level. Therefore, we recommend including the following key elements: Strengthening EU Global Health Leadership and External Action ● Medicine shortages are a global challenge that cannot be addressed in isolation. To strengthen its own health security, the EU should incorporate measures that strengthen international collaboration in medicine production and R&D, particularly with Africa. ● The Global Gateway initiative, Horizon Europe, and the European and Developing Countries Clinical Trials Partnership (EDCTP) should be aligned with the Critical Medicines Act to enhance global R&D efforts, technology transfer, regulatory approval, manufacturing and distribution
context_after: ● The EU-Africa Global Health Partnership and the Team Europe Initiative (TEI) on Manufacturing and Access to Vaccines, Medicines, and Health Technologies (MAV+) should be leveraged to support joint manufacturing capabilities, regulatory harmonization, and pandemic preparedness. Synergies should also be built between the TEI on Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) and MAV+, to support manufacturing and effective supplies of sexual and reproductive health (SRH) tools, including contraceptives ● Support for regional health initiatives such as the Africa CDC’s Partnership for African Health Products Manufacturing (PHAHM) should be integrated into the Act to build long-term sustainability. ● The Act should be fully in line with the goals and ambitions of the EU Global Health Strategy, ensuring a strong EU’s role in promoting global health security. Recommendation: The Critical Medicines Act should explicitly recognize the EU’s role in strengthening global health security, particularly through investments in research and pharmaceutical production in partner countries.
article_text:
Article 26
Tasks of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.The Critical Medicines Group shall facilitate coordination in the implementation of this Regulation and, where appropriate, advise the Commission, so as to maximise the impact of the measures envisaged and to avoid any unintended effects on the internal market. 2.In order to attain the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Critical Medicines Group shall perform the following tasks: (a)facilitate coordination on strategic orientation of the financial support for strategic projects, including by exchanging information on the manufacturing capacity for a given critical medicinal product, existing or planned, in the Member States and facilitate discussion on the capacity needed in the Union to strengthen its supply security and availability of critical medicinal products within the Union; (b)facilitate exchanges on the national programmes referred to in Article 19 and enable cooperation on and coordination of Member States public procurement policies with regard to critical medicinal products; (c)facilitate discussion of the need for a collaborative procurement initiative for a given medicinal product; (d)advise the MSSG to provide the order of priority of critical medicinal products for vulnerability evaluation, and propose a review or an update of existing evaluations where necessary. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall enable the exchanges of information between the Member States and the Commission as referred to in Article 17 and shall enable, where necessary, a coordination of respective actions aiming to attain the objectives of this Regulation. 4.The Critical Medicines Group shall periodically discuss the potential contribution of strategic partnerships to the objectives of this Regulation, prioritisation of third countries for this purpose, and the consistency and potential synergies between Member States’ cooperation with relevant third countries and the actions carried out by the Union. 5.The Critical Medicines Group, at the Commission’s request, may provide an opinion on matters related to the application of this Regulation in the context of performing tasks as referred to in this Article. Chapter VI International cooperation
provision_effect: Establishes the Critical Medicines Coordination Group's tasks, including facilitating coordination on strategic projects, procurement policies, collaborative procurement, vulnerability evaluations, and discussing strategic partnerships with third countries.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: DSW explicitly advocates for international collaboration and strategic partnerships, particularly with third countries (e.g., Africa), to address medicine shortages. Article 26(4) directly tasks the Critical Medicines Group with periodically discussing the contribution of strategic partnerships to the regulation's objectives, prioritisation of third countries, and synergies between Member States' cooperation with third countries and EU actions — which responds to DSW's call for incorporating international collaboration measures into the Act.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2025)1585600 - 27/02/2025 DSW submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Critical Medicines Act The Critical Medicines Act presents an important opportunity to enhance the resilience of Europe’s pharmaceutical supply chains. However, to ensure a truly effective Critical Medicines Act, we urge the Commission to adopt a global, inclusive, and forward-looking approach that reflects the EU’s responsibility in global health security. Integrating global health R&D, gender-sensitive policies, and sustainable procurement models will ensure that the Act not only safeguards EU medicine security but also contributes to a healthier and more equitable world. At the same time, this will help to further strengthen health security at EU level. Therefore, we recommend including the following key elements: Strengthening EU Global Health Leadership and External Action ● Medicine shortages are a global challenge that cannot be addressed in isolation. To strengthen its own health security, the EU should incorporate measures that strengthen international collaboration in medicine production and R&D, particularly with Africa. ● The Global Gateway initiative, Horizon Europe, and the European and Developing Countries Clinical Trials Partnership (EDCTP) should be aligned with the Critical Medicines Act to enhance global R&D efforts, technology transfer, regulatory approval, manufacturing and distribution
context_after: ● The EU-Africa Global Health Partnership and the Team Europe Initiative (TEI) on Manufacturing and Access to Vaccines, Medicines, and Health Technologies (MAV+) should be leveraged to support joint manufacturing capabilities, regulatory harmonization, and pandemic preparedness. Synergies should also be built between the TEI on Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) and MAV+, to support manufacturing and effective supplies of sexual and reproductive health (SRH) tools, including contraceptives ● Support for regional health initiatives such as the Africa CDC’s Partnership for African Health Products Manufacturing (PHAHM) should be integrated into the Act to build long-term sustainability. ● The Act should be fully in line with the goals and ambitions of the EU Global Health Strategy, ensuring a strong EU’s role in promoting global health security. Recommendation: The Critical Medicines Act should explicitly recognize the EU’s role in strengthening global health security, particularly through investments in research and pharmaceutical production in partner countries.
article_text:
Article 27
Strategic partnerships Without prejudice to the prerogatives of the Council, the Commission, shall explore possibilities of concluding strategic partnerships aiming to diversify sourcing of critical medicinal products, their active substances and key inputs to increase the security of supply of critical medicinal products in the Union. The Commission shall also explore the possibility of building on existing forms of cooperation, when possible, to support security of supply and reinforce efforts to strengthen the production of critical medicinal products in the Union. Chapter VII Amendments to Regulation (EU) 2024/795
provision_effect: Requires the Commission to explore strategic partnerships to diversify sourcing of critical medicinal products and strengthen supply security in the Union.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: DSW explicitly advocates for the EU to incorporate measures strengthening international collaboration in medicine production and R&D, particularly with Africa, and to align global initiatives with the Critical Medicines Act. Article 27 directly responds to this by mandating the Commission to explore strategic partnerships aimed at diversifying sourcing and building on existing cooperation frameworks — concrete action in the same direction DSW advocated for.
