article_text:
Article 2
Scope 1.This Regulation applies to the critical medicinal products listed in the Union List of Critical Medicinal Products referred to in Article 131 of Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]. 2.Chapter IV and Article 26(2) point (c) also apply to medicinal products of common interest. Chapter III does not apply to medicinal products of common interest.
provision_effect: Defines the scope of the Regulation by applying it to critical medicinal products listed in the Union List, while specifying which chapters also apply to medicinal products of common interest.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: EFA specifically advocates for clear and precise criteria to determine whether a medicinal product qualifies as being "of common interest" for scoping purposes. The provision does reference "medicinal products of common interest" and differentiates their treatment under various chapters, but it does not itself define or clarify the criteria for that classification — it merely applies the scope. It is therefore genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies EFA's ask for transparent, well-defined criteria, as the definitional work appears to reside elsewhere (e.g., in the referenced Regulation COM(2023) 193 final).

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: 3. Clear identification of issues to avoid duplication Given the existing measures and policy proposals the revision of the pharmaceutical legislation will introduce and enforce, the EU’s toolbox for addressing supply security for medicines is already extensive. In 2023, the European Commission set out a package of short-term and medium/longer- term actions to prevent or mitigate critical shortages at the EU level, focusing on the most critical medicines for which supply needs to always be assured in the EU. The CMA could bring great consistency and certainty regarding its evolution, although a regulatory approach could present challenges in terms of flexibility and penalties. It will be key that the objectives of the CMA are clear and do not duplicate those of existing legislations or other policy efforts and initiatives that are already demonstrating results. Many of the tools that could be included within a CMA (the list of critical medicines, a process for assessing vulnerability, risk assessment and reporting requirements, and encouragement of strategic projects) are already underway for medicines. It will be fundamental that the CMA does not create additional layers of complexities to an already complex supply chain ecosystem and that the impact of the CMA is not jeopardized by heterogenous, burdensome and contrasting initiatives/requirements from Member States. 4. Establishing high-level industry benchmarks and targets may send wrong signals
context_before: 1 EFPIA (2020). EU strategic resilience in pharmaceuticals: global value chains and innovation. Available at: https://www.efpia.eu/news- events/the-efpia-view/blog-articles/eu-strategic-resilience-in-pharmaceuticals-global-value-chains-and-innovation/. www.efpia.e 1 u
context_after: 4. Establishing high-level industry benchmarks and targets may send wrong signals. Based on examples from the CRMA and the Chips Act, a CMA could establish benchmarks and targets for localising production and diversifying sourcing of APIs and intermediate and finished products for medicines on the Union List of Critical Medicines. This will send signals regarding the insularity of the European markets. Benchmarks and targets could signal a protectionist stance and may lower the appetite for private-sector investments in innovation and impact on EU’s innovation landscape and global competitiveness. Benchmarks and targets may not be realistic and may create market uncertainties for marketing authorisation holders. The manner in which these measures are implemented will determine whether the external agenda promotes regional collaboration or veers toward disruptive actions, such as export bans. The industry is concerned about how these tools could be used without properly assessing potential supply disruptions at international, European, and national levels. 5. Calls to localise supply chain and production capacity must be carefully reconsidered in the ongoing CMA debate. A CMA geared towards increased localisation of production of certain essential medicines (not necessarily innovative) in the EU should carefully consider its impact on global supply chains as it could distort the global market in several ways
article_text:
Article 3
Definitions For the purpose of this Regulation, the following definitions shall apply: (1) ‘medicinal product’ means a medicinal product as defined in Article 4 point (1) of Directive (EU) …/… of the European Parliament and of the Council [reference to be added to corresponding Article after adoption of cf. COM(2023) 192 final]; (2) ‘key input’ means input material other than an active substance required in the manufacturing process of a given medicinal product, including primary packaging materials, excipients, solvents and reagents; (3) ‘active substance’ means an active substance as defined in Article 4 point (3) of Directive (EU) …/… [reference to be added to corresponding Article after adoption of cf. COM(2023) 192 final]; (4) ‘critical medicinal product’ means a medicinal product for which insufficient supply results in serious harm or risk of serious harm to patients as defined in Article 4 point (13) of Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]; (5) ‘medicinal product of common interest’ means a medicinal product, other than a critical medicinal product, for which in three or more Member States the functioning of the market does not sufficiently ensure the availability and accessibility to patients in the quantities and presentations necessary to cover the needs of patients in those Member States; (6) ‘vulnerability in the supply chains’ means risks and weaknesses within the supply chains of critical medicinal products, identified at the aggregated level, taking into account all authorised medicinal products in the EU and grouped under a common name with the same route of administration and formulation, that compromise the continuous supply of such medicinal products to patients in the Union; (7) ‘vulnerability evaluation’ means the evaluation of the supply chains of critical medicinal products to identify their vulnerabilities performed by the MSSG in accordance with Regulation (EU) …/… of the European Parliament and of the Council [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]; (8) ‘common name’ means a common name as defined in Article 4 point (48) of Directive (EU) …/… of the European Parliament and of the Council [reference to be added to corresponding Article after adoption of cf. COM(2023) 192 final]; (9) ‘contracting authorities’ means contracting authorities as defined in Article 2(1) point (1) of Directive 2014/24/EU; (10) ‘strategic project’ means an industrial project identified pursuant to the criteria set out in Article 5; (11) ‘project promoter’ means any undertaking or consortium of undertakings developing a strategic project; (12) ‘permit granting process’ means a process covering all relevant permits to build and operate a strategic project, including building, chemical and grid connection permits and environmental assessments and authorisations where those are required and encompassing all applications and procedures; (13) ‘innovative manufacturing process’ means a novel manufacturing process and technology or novel application of an existing technology, including, but not limited to, decentralised manufacturing, continuous manufacturing, Artificial Intelligence, platform techniques, 3D manufacturing; (15) ‘Member States’ cross-border procurement’ means a procurement procedure initiated between the contracting authorities from different Member States on the basis of Article 39 of Directive 2014/24/EC; (16) ‘procurement on behalf of or in the name of the Member States’ means a procurement procedure initiated at the request of Member States and mandating the Commission to act as a central purchasing body on behalf of, or in the name of, the requesting Member States, as provided for in Article 168(3) of Regulation (EU) 2024/2509; (17) ‘joint procurement’ means a procurement procedure carried out jointly by the Commission and Member States, as provided for in Article 168(2) of Regulation (EU) 2024/2509; (18) ‘supplier’ means the manufacturer or marketing authorisation holder of finished dosage forms, or manufacturer of key inputs or active substances; (19) ‘strategic partnership’ means a commitment between the Union and a third country, group of third countries or international organisations to increase cooperation related to one or more critical medicinal products that is established through a non-binding instrument and which facilitates beneficial outcomes for both the Union and the relevant third country, group of third countries or international organisation. Chapter II Strengthening the Union’s security of supply
provision_effect: Establishes a set of definitions for key terms used in the Regulation, including 'critical medicinal product', 'vulnerability in the supply chains', 'vulnerability evaluation', 'strategic project', and others.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org's position specifically warns against duplication of existing tools (such as lists of critical medicines, vulnerability assessments, and strategic projects) and calls for clarity and consistency in the CMA's objectives. Article 3 defines these very concepts (e.g., 'critical medicinal product', 'vulnerability evaluation', 'strategic project'), which are directly relevant to the org's concerns. However, since this article only provides definitions rather than establishing substantive obligations or mechanisms, it is genuinely unclear whether the definitions as drafted satisfy, contradict, or ignore the org's advocacy for non-duplication and clarity — a real connection exists but the direction cannot be confidently determined from definitions alone.

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: 2. Incentivise innovative technologies and supporting infrastructure that can help to address supply chain vulnerabilities. These technologies include continuous manufacturing (for both active ingredients and medicinal products), portable, modular manufacturing facilities, and single-use-systems that enable faster and agile manufacturing. 3. Streamline regulatory processes and extensive supply requirements, including by applying modern digital technologies to regulatory processes and minimising variations for manufacturing change authorisations (i.e. type 2 variations). 4. End national stockpiling requirements at finished product level and, as alternatives: a. To ensure that safety stockpiles are effective and proportional to their purpose, establish maximum thresholds for the entire EU region, including specific thresholds by product categories according to the identified vulnerabilities of critical medicines’ supply chains; and b. Leverage EU solidarity mechanisms between Member States that would allow marketing authorisation holders to quickly redeploy inventories, therefore avoiding uneven distribution of stocks. 5. Include a guidance on medicines public procurement that integrates inter alia security of supply, multi-winner tenders, MEAT (Most Economically Advantageous Tender) criteria, and codifying this guidance in the Public Procurement Directive when it is revised. 6
context_before: Implications for the policy debate on the Critical Medicines Act The European research-based industry significantly contributed to the response to the COVID-19 pandemic by developing new vaccines and innovative therapeutics at record speed; rapidly adapting complex global supply chains in an unstable geopolitical environment; and swiftly reallocating supplies to meet unprecedented demand changes. Considering lessons learned from this experience, our industry supports the European Commission’s objective to stimulate innovation in Europe and a sustainable and resilient supply chain. Taking into account the considerations mentioned above, we believe a CMA should: 1. Require that competent authorities responsible for enacting, implementing, and enforcing provisions of existing and future EU legislations on food, chemical, and environmental protection, conduct impact assessments evaluating to what extent these provisions affect the security of supply of critical medicines in the EU. These assessments should be complemented at national level with an evaluation of the impact of economic policies, such as cost-containment measures, on the sustainability of supply. 2. Incentivise innovative technologies and supporting infrastructure that can help to address supply chain vulnerabilities
context_after: 6. Establish open trade agreements with partner countries to i) facilitate cross-border movement of medicines and vaccines, ii) diversify supply networks including for raw materials and equipment for manufacturing, iii) remove barriers to trade, iv) strengthen regulatory cooperation, and v) collaborate to safeguard innovation.
article_text:
Article 5
Strategic Projects A project located in the Union and related to creating or increasing manufacturing capacity shall be considered as a strategic project if it meets at least one of the following criteria: (a)it creates or increases manufacturing capacity for one or more critical medicinal products or for collecting or manufacturing their active substances; (b)it modernises an existing manufacturing site for one or more critical medicinal products or their active substances to ensure greater sustainability or increased efficiency; (c)it creates or increases manufacturing capacity for key inputs necessary for the manufacturing of one or more critical medicinal products or their active substances; (d)it contributes to the roll-out of a technology that plays a key role in enabling the manufacturing of one or more critical medicinal products, their active substances or key inputs.
provision_effect: Establishes criteria for designating manufacturing projects as 'Strategic Projects', including projects that create/increase manufacturing capacity, modernise existing sites, or contribute to key enabling technologies for critical medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The org explicitly advocates for incentivising innovative technologies (continuous manufacturing, modular/portable facilities, single-use systems) and supporting infrastructure to address supply chain vulnerabilities. Article 5(d) specifically designates as strategic projects those that contribute to the roll-out of technologies enabling manufacturing of critical medicinal products, directly responding to the org's call to incentivise such innovative manufacturing technologies. The provision takes concrete action in the direction the org advocated.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2025)1513853 - 25/02/2025 (25) EU 2 25 February 2025 FEFAC CONTRIBUTION TO THE CALL FOR EVIDENCE REGARDING THE CRITICAL MEDICINES ACT FEFAC representing the EU compound feed and premixtures sector would like to take the opportunity of this consultation on a critical medicines act to remind that in the context of the One-Health policy, the question of availability of medicines should be considered simultaneously both for human and animal care. The resilience of the EU livestock chain and the health and welfare of the animals depends on access to veterinary medicines and also to essential nutritional feed additives. FEFAC wants to draw herewith the attention to huge similarities between the situation of the human medicines sector and the veterinary medicines & feed additives sector, with an increasing EU dependency and extreme vulnerability vis-à-vis certain Third Countries for access to essential substances such as vitamins and amino acids. It is urgent that the EU authorities realise the dramatic consequences for the EU livestock sector in case of rupture of supply. We therefore call for an ambitious plan at EU level to support investments in essential feed additives manufacturing capacity within Europe for vitamins and amino acids to mitigate the EU dependency vis-à-vis third countries for these substances
context_after: FEFAC aisbl – Fédération Européenne des Fabricants d’Aliment Composés Rue de la Loi 223, Bte 3 B-1040 Bruxelles Tel : +32 (0)2 285 00 50 Fax : +32 (0) 2 230 57 22 fefac@fefac.eu www.fefac.eu EU Transparency Register: 77105321408-83
article_text:
Article 15
Financial support by Member States 1.Without prejudice to Articles 107 and 108 TFEU, Member States may prioritise financial support to strategic projects that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products identified following a vulnerability evaluation and with due consideration to the strategic orientations of the Critical Medicines Group referred to in Article 26(2) point (a). 2.For as long as the critical medicinal product is on the Union List of Critical Medicinal Products, an undertaking that has benefitted from financial support for a strategic project shall prioritise supply to the Union market and use its very best efforts to ensure that the critical medicinal product remains available in the Member States where it is being marketed. 3.The Member State that provided financial support to a strategic project may request such undertaking to provide the necessary supplies of a critical medicinal product, active substance or key inputs, as applicable, to the Union market to avoid shortages in one or several Member States. Any Member State that encounters a threat of shortages of the critical medicinal product in question may demand the Member State that provided financial support to submit a request on its behalf.
provision_effect: Allows Member States to prioritise financial support to strategic projects addressing supply chain vulnerabilities in critical medicinal products, with obligations on supported undertakings to prioritise supply to the Union market.
label: NOISE
reasoning: FEFAC's advocacy is focused on extending EU-level investment support to veterinary medicines and feed additives (vitamins, amino acids), which fall outside the scope of this provision concerning human critical medicinal products. The provision deals exclusively with financial support for strategic projects related to critical medicinal products for human use, while FEFAC's position concerns a different sector (animal feed additives). There is no substantive advocacy position on the specific mechanism this article establishes.

article_text:
Article 5
Strategic Projects A project located in the Union and related to creating or increasing manufacturing capacity shall be considered as a strategic project if it meets at least one of the following criteria: (a)it creates or increases manufacturing capacity for one or more critical medicinal products or for collecting or manufacturing their active substances; (b)it modernises an existing manufacturing site for one or more critical medicinal products or their active substances to ensure greater sustainability or increased efficiency; (c)it creates or increases manufacturing capacity for key inputs necessary for the manufacturing of one or more critical medicinal products or their active substances; (d)it contributes to the roll-out of a technology that plays a key role in enabling the manufacturing of one or more critical medicinal products, their active substances or key inputs.
provision_effect: Establishes criteria for designating manufacturing projects as 'Strategic Projects', including projects that create/increase manufacturing capacity, modernise existing sites, or contribute to key enabling technologies for critical medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The org explicitly advocates for incentivising innovative technologies (continuous manufacturing, modular/portable facilities, single-use systems) and supporting infrastructure to address supply chain vulnerabilities. Article 5(d) specifically designates as strategic projects those that contribute to the roll-out of technologies enabling manufacturing of critical medicinal products, directly responding to the org's call to incentivise such innovative manufacturing technologies. The provision takes concrete action in the direction the org advocated.

source_text: European Healthcare Distribution Association the vital link in healthcare Groupement International de la Répartition Pharmaceutique b. The reporting of stock levels for critical medicines under the stockpiling obligation can be mandated if required by public health authorities. Before reporting, MAHs and/or outsourced third parties must receive assurances that their IP protected data and trading information will not be compromised. Conclusion The Critical Medicines Act represents a crucial opportunity to strengthen the resilience of Europe’s pharmaceutical supply chain by addressing the root causes of supply disruptions—most notably, the complexity and vulnerabilities within upstream supply chains. Pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers play a pivotal role in ensuring that once medicines are made available, they reach patients efficiently and equitably. As the backbone of medicine distribution, pharmaceutical full- line wholesalers provide cost-effective, reliable, and fair access across all regions, ensuring that no patient is left without essential treatments. The Act must be developed in close collaboration with supply chain actors—including pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers, manufacturers, healthcare providers, and regulatory authorities—to ensure that proposed measures are both practical and effective. Rue de la Loi 82 E girp@girp.eu 1040 Brussels, Belgium W www.girp.eu
context_before: The service fee should cover the costs of stockpiling critical medicines. b. Any further activities (e.g. distribution to pharmacies or hospitals) should not be considered as part of this service fee. We consider this important, as for example direct-to-pharmacy distribution is not the most economically efficient way to distribute medicines, and we cannot support healthcare budgets being spent so inefficiently for critical medicines. 4) Duration of stockpiling a. The maximum duration of stockpiling should be harmonised and not exceed a reasonable period compared to normal demand, e.g. three months. Shorter or longer time periods are possible by exception only for a very limited range of products depending on their shelf-life and other quality requirements (e.g. stability). 5) Stock rotation and release a. FEFO (First Expire First Out) stock rotation principle should apply to stockpiled medicines. b. MAHs shall take responsibility for expired stock. c. Emergency stock shall only be released when requested by the responsible public health authority. 6) Oversight a. Public health authorities shall be responsible for overseeing national stockpiling obligations. Rue de la Loi 82 E girp@girp.eu 1040 Brussels, Belgium W www.girp.eu
article_text:
Article 17
Exchange of information on funded projects 1.Member States shall inform the Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘the Critical Medicines Group’) referred to in Article 24 of the intention to provide financial support to strategic projects sufficiently in advance to allow the group to carry out its coordination task as set out in Article 25. 2.The Commission shall inform periodically the Critical Medicines Group of the strategic projects that benefited from financial support from the Union. The Commission may inform the Critical Medicines Group of the intention to propose the establishment of funding possibilities specifically designed to address vulnerabilities in the supply chains as well as inform of any other programmes that may benefit the availability of critical medicinal products, under specific rules and conditions of these Union funding programmes. Chapter IV Demand side measures Section I Award criteria and other procurement requirements and related measures
provision_effect: Establishes a requirement for Member States and the Commission to inform the Critical Medicines Coordination Group about funded strategic projects and potential funding programmes addressing supply chain vulnerabilities.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text provided is largely a concluding statement emphasising the role of pharmaceutical wholesalers and a general call for collaboration with supply chain actors, without expressing a specific advocacy position on the exchange of information about funded projects or the coordination group's role in that process. The text does not take a directional stance on the mechanism established by Article 17.

article_text:
Article 22
Commission procurement on behalf of or in the name of Member States 1.By way of derogation from Article 168(3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509 where nine or more Member States jointly request the Commission to procure on their behalf, or in their name, the Commission may initiate a procurement procedure under the conditions set out in this Article when the procurement relates to medicinal products belonging to one of the following categories below; (a)critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability evaluation has identified a vulnerability in the supply chains or for which the MSSG has recommended a common procurement initiative; (b)medicinal products of common interest, for which a joint clinical assessment report has been published pursuant to Article 12(4) Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 of the European Parliament and the Council , or which have undergone a clinical assessment carried out under the voluntary cooperation among Member States as per Article 23(1) point (e) of that Regulation. 2.The joint request referred to in paragraph 1 shall only be made where the medicinal product concerned fulfils one of the criteria set out in that paragraph and if the requested procurement procedure will help to improve the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the Union or ensure the availability and accessibility of medicinal products of common interest, as applicable. 3.The participation in the procurement procedure shall be open to all Member States. The Commission shall inform all Member States of the request, through the Critical Medicines Group, and invite them to join the procedure. 4.The Commission shall assess the utility, necessity and proportionality of the request and whether the request is justified in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall in particular verify whether the procurement could constitute discrimination or restriction to trade or a distortion to competition. 5.The Commission shall inform the interested Member States within one month of the request of its decision and state its reasons in case of a refusal. 6.If in light of the Commission assessment, it is necessary, in order to achieve the objectives of this Regulation, to conduct the procurement as exclusive for the Member States or to agree to minimum binding quantities, the Commission agreement to pursue the procedure may be conditioned upon acceptance of these conditions by interested Member States. 7.Except for the derogations provided for in this Regulation, the procurement referred to in this Article shall be carried out in accordance with Article 168 (3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing the Commission to conduct joint procurement of critical or common-interest medicinal products on behalf of nine or more requesting Member States, subject to conditions of utility, necessity, proportionality, and non-discrimination.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text does not express any specific advocacy position on joint Commission procurement mechanisms. The excerpted position focuses on stockpiling reporting obligations, IP data protection, and a general call for supply chain collaboration — none of which directly address or take a stance on the Commission's procurement-on-behalf-of-Member-States procedure established in Article 22.

article_text:
Article 23
Joint Procurement 1.Under conditions laid down in this Article and by way of derogation from Article 168(2) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509, if a contract is necessary for the implementation of the joint action between the Commission and Member States, the Commission and at least nine Member States may engage, as contracting parties, in a joint procurement procedure. 2.A joint procurement procedure may be organised following a request by the Member States or at the Commission’s initiative when the procurement relates to medicinal products belonging to one of the categories below: (a)critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability evaluation has identified a vulnerability in the supply chains or for which the MSSG has recommended a common procurement initiative; (b)medicinal products of common interest, for which a joint clinical assessment report has been published pursuant to Article 12(4) Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 of the European Parliament and the Council , or which have undergone a clinical assessment carried out under the voluntary cooperation among Member States as per Article 23(1) point (e) of that Regulation. 3.The Commission may decide to conduct the joint procurement procedure if the procurement procedure helps to improve the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the Union or ensure the availability and accessibility of medicinal products of common interest, as applicable. 4.The participation in the procurement procedure shall be open to all Member States. The Commission shall inform all Member States of the request through the Critical Medicines Group and invite them to join the procedure. 5.The Commission shall assess the necessity of a joint action and whether the request is justified in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall in particular verify whether the procurement could constitute discrimination or restriction to trade or a distortion to competition. 6.If in light of the Commission assessment, it is necessary, in order to achieve the objectives of this Regulation, to conduct the procurement as exclusive for the Member States or to agree to minimum binding quantities, the Commission agreement to pursue the procedure may be conditioned upon acceptance of these conditions by interested Member States. 7.The Commission shall inform the interested Member States within one month of the request of its decision and state its reasons in case of a refusal. 8.Except for the derogations provided for in this Regulation, the joint procurement procedure shall be carried out by the Commission in accordance with Article 168 (2) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509.
provision_effect: Establishes a joint procurement procedure between the Commission and Member States for critical medicinal products and medicinal products of common interest, including conditions, eligibility, and governance of such procurement.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text does not express any specific advocacy position on joint procurement mechanisms. The excerpted position focuses on stock-level reporting obligations, IP data protection, and the general role of pharmaceutical wholesalers in the supply chain. The concluding remarks are high-level endorsements of collaboration with supply chain actors, without any directional stance on joint procurement.

article_text:
Article 26
Tasks of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.The Critical Medicines Group shall facilitate coordination in the implementation of this Regulation and, where appropriate, advise the Commission, so as to maximise the impact of the measures envisaged and to avoid any unintended effects on the internal market. 2.In order to attain the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Critical Medicines Group shall perform the following tasks: (a)facilitate coordination on strategic orientation of the financial support for strategic projects, including by exchanging information on the manufacturing capacity for a given critical medicinal product, existing or planned, in the Member States and facilitate discussion on the capacity needed in the Union to strengthen its supply security and availability of critical medicinal products within the Union; (b)facilitate exchanges on the national programmes referred to in Article 19 and enable cooperation on and coordination of Member States public procurement policies with regard to critical medicinal products; (c)facilitate discussion of the need for a collaborative procurement initiative for a given medicinal product; (d)advise the MSSG to provide the order of priority of critical medicinal products for vulnerability evaluation, and propose a review or an update of existing evaluations where necessary. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall enable the exchanges of information between the Member States and the Commission as referred to in Article 17 and shall enable, where necessary, a coordination of respective actions aiming to attain the objectives of this Regulation. 4.The Critical Medicines Group shall periodically discuss the potential contribution of strategic partnerships to the objectives of this Regulation, prioritisation of third countries for this purpose, and the consistency and potential synergies between Member States’ cooperation with relevant third countries and the actions carried out by the Union. 5.The Critical Medicines Group, at the Commission’s request, may provide an opinion on matters related to the application of this Regulation in the context of performing tasks as referred to in this Article. Chapter VI International cooperation
provision_effect: Establishes the tasks of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group, including facilitating coordination on strategic orientation, financial support, procurement policies, collaborative procurement initiatives, and strategic partnerships for critical medicinal products.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text provided is largely a concluding statement endorsing the Critical Medicines Act and calling for collaboration with supply chain actors, including wholesalers — this is a general endorsement without a specific directional stance on the tasks or structure of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group. The specific point raised (IP protection for stock-level reporting) does not relate to the provision's subject matter. There is no substantive advocacy position that can be evaluated against what Article 26 specifically establishes.

article_text:
Article 29
Obligation of the market actors to provide information 1.Marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators in the supply and distribution chains of critical medicinal products including their key inputs and active substances or medicinal products of common interest shall upon request provide the Commission or national authorities, as relevant, the requested information necessary for the purpose of application of this Regulation. 2.The Commission and national authorities of the Member States shall aim to avoid duplication of the information requested and submitted. 3.The Commission and national authorities of the Member States shall assess the merits of duly substantiated confidentiality claims made by marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators, requested to provide information per paragraph 1, and shall protect any information that is commercially confidential against unjustified disclosure.
provision_effect: Requires marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators in the supply chain of critical medicinal products to provide information upon request to the Commission or national authorities, while obliging those authorities to avoid duplication of requests and to protect commercially confidential information against unjustified disclosure.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: GIRP explicitly conditions acceptance of mandatory stock-level reporting on assurances that IP-protected data and trading information will not be compromised. Article 29(3) directly responds to this concern by requiring authorities to assess confidentiality claims and protect commercially sensitive information against unjustified disclosure, while Article 29(2) addresses the burden of duplication — both provisions act in the direction GIRP advocated.

source_text: • Support for "First-of-a-Kind" Manufacturing: Europe cannot afford to lose its production sites for critical medicines. The Act must incentivize cutting-edge production facilities, enabling automation, continuous manufacturing, and environmental compliance while ensuring long- term security. • Targeted Regional & Financial Aid: The EU should unlock regional funding for industrial parks and embed health security criteria into financial mechanisms like EU4Health. • Dedicated funding for Critical Medicines Act via the MFF. 2. Stop Market Failures Driving Medicine Shortages Europe’s medicine shortages are due to pricing and procurement policies that prioritize cost- cutting over security. The Critical Medicines Act must: • Mandate Secure & Strategic Procurement: Nearly 40% of all medicines in Europe are purchased through tenders. Current procurement models focusing on price-only undermine supply security. The Act must enforce MEAT criteria, ensuring that supply chain resilience, sustainability, and reliability become mandatory procurement criteria. • Fix Broken Pricing Models: Inflexible pricing regulations cripple manufacturers during inflation and rising costs. The EU must: Implement dynamic pricing mechanisms to prevent critical medicines from o disappearing. Encourage national pricing reforms that allow for inflation adjustments. o Enforce the Transparency Directive to align medicine pricing with long-term supply o security. 3

Ares(2025)1534776 - 26/02/2025 The Critical Medicines Act must lead to a resilient, competitive, and sustainable off-patent pharmaceutical industry. It must ensure secure supply chains, reward investment and strengthen Europe’s autonomy in critical medicines. 1. Invest in Resilient Manufacturing The EU must reverse the dangerous decline in local FDF and API production for off-patent medicines, including critical medicines, biosimilars and other products of essential interest. A secure off-patent sector requires: • A reformed State Aid & IPCEI framework: Current policies fail to support essential off-patent medicines, which make up 90% of Europe’s critical medicine list. The Act must broaden the definition of innovation to include advanced manufacturing, automation, and sustainability, ensuring vital investment into this sector. • Support for "First-of-a-Kind" Manufacturing: Europe cannot afford to lose its production sites for critical medicines
context_after: Encourage national pricing reforms that allow for inflation adjustments. o Enforce the Transparency Directive to align medicine pricing with long-term supply o security. 3. Establish a European Solidarity Stock System National stockpiling rules are disrupting supply chains and worsening medicine shortages. Instead of uncoordinated national hoarding, the EU must: • Foster the European Solidarity Mechanism that allows efficient medicine redistribution between member states during crises. • Improve monitoring and stock allocation through existing tools such as EMVS. • Foster reallocation of products through regulatory simplification tools such as e-leaflet, muti- country and EU packs. Allow for the same level of flexibility for MAHs in stock reallocation as it granted to parallel traders.
article_text:
Article 1
Objectives and subject matter 1.The objective of this Regulation is to strengthen the security of supply and the availability of critical medicinal products within the Union, thereby ensuring a high level of public health protection and supporting the security of the Union. The objective of this Regulation is also to improve the availability and accessibility of other medicinal products, where the functioning of the market does not otherwise sufficiently ensure the availability and accessibility of those medicinal products to patients, whilst giving due consideration to the appropriateness to ensure the affordability of medicinal products. 2.To achieve the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Regulation sets out a framework to: (a)facilitate investments in manufacturing capacity for critical medicinal products, their active substances and other key inputs in the Union; (b)lower the risk of supply disruptions and strengthen availability by incentivising supply chain diversification and resilience in the public procurement procedures of critical medicinal products and other medicinal products of common interest; (c)leverage the aggregated demand of participating Member States through collaborative procurement procedures, and (d)support the diversification of supply chains also by facilitating the conclusion of strategic partnerships.
provision_effect: Establishes the objectives and framework of the Regulation, including facilitating investments in manufacturing capacity, incentivising supply chain diversification and resilience in procurement, leveraging aggregated demand through collaborative procurement, and supporting strategic partnerships for critical medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for incentivising cutting-edge manufacturing facilities, mandating supply chain resilience and MEAT criteria in procurement, and dedicated funding mechanisms — all of which are directly reflected in the provision's framework objectives (facilitating manufacturing investment, incentivising supply chain diversification in procurement, and collaborative procurement procedures). The provision takes concrete action in the same direction as the org's specific asks on manufacturing support and procurement reform.

article_text:
Article 4
Strategic objective of the Union 1.The security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products for patients is a strategic objective of the Union. 2.The Member States and the Commission shall work together to strengthen the security of supply and continuous availability of critical medicinal products in the Union through measures that take full advantage of the potential of the internal market. 3.The Commission shall support the coordinated efforts of the Members States. Chapter III Enabling conditions for investment Section I Criteria and procedure for the recognition of strategic projects
provision_effect: Establishes security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products as a strategic objective of the Union, requiring Member States and the Commission to work together to strengthen supply security through internal market measures.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Insud Pharma explicitly advocates for the Critical Medicines Act to address supply security through targeted investment, procurement reform, and pricing mechanisms — all aimed at ensuring long-term security of supply for critical medicines. The provision directly establishes security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products as a strategic Union objective and mandates coordinated action, which aligns with the direction Insud Pharma advocates for, even if the provision does not address every specific ask (e.g., MEAT criteria or dynamic pricing).

The Act must enforce MEAT criteria, ensuring that supply chain resilience, sustainability, and reliability become mandatory procurement criteria. • Fix Broken Pricing Models: Inflexible pricing regulations cripple manufacturers during inflation and rising costs. The EU must: Implement dynamic pricing mechanisms to prevent critical medicines from o disappearing. Encourage national pricing reforms that allow for inflation adjustments. o Enforce the Transparency Directive to align medicine pricing with long-term supply o security. 3

Article 15
Financial support by Member States 1.Without prejudice to Articles 107 and 108 TFEU, Member States may prioritise financial support to strategic projects that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products identified following a vulnerability evaluation and with due consideration to the strategic orientations of the Critical Medicines Group referred to in Article 26(2) point (a). 2.For as long as the critical medicinal product is on the Union List of Critical Medicinal Products, an undertaking that has benefitted from financial support for a strategic project shall prioritise supply to the Union market and use its very best efforts to ensure that the critical medicinal product remains available in the Member States where it is being marketed. 3.The Member State that provided financial support to a strategic project may request such undertaking to provide the necessary supplies of a critical medicinal product, active substance or key inputs, as applicable, to the Union market to avoid shortages in one or several Member States. Any Member State that encounters a threat of shortages of the critical medicinal product in question may demand the Member State that provided financial support to submit a request on its behalf.
provision_effect: Allows Member States to prioritise financial support to strategic projects addressing supply chain vulnerabilities for critical medicinal products, and requires beneficiaries to prioritise supply to the Union market and respond to shortage-prevention requests.

Article 16
Financial support from the Union 1.For the duration of the Multiannual Financial Framework 2021-2027 strategic projects may be supported by Union funding, including but not limited to such Union programmes as the EU4Health Programme , Horizon Europe , and the Digital Europe Programme provided that such support is in line with the objectives set out in the regulations establishing those programmes. 2.At the request of a project promoter, justified by necessity to provide results of vulnerability evaluation for the purpose of an application for Union funding, the designated authority shall assess whether a strategic project addresses a vulnerability in the supply chains identified following the vulnerability evaluation. The designated authority shall provide its assessment to a project promoter within 15 working days of its request. The designated authority shall inform the Commission about the strategic projects identified as addressing an existing vulnerability in the supply chains without delay.
provision_effect: Establishes that strategic projects may receive Union funding from programmes such as EU4Health, Horizon Europe, and Digital Europe, and sets up a process for designated authorities to assess whether strategic projects address supply chain vulnerabilities for the purpose of Union funding applications.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Insud Pharma explicitly advocates for embedding health security criteria into financial mechanisms like EU4Health and for dedicated funding for critical medicines via the MFF. Article 16 directly delivers on this by enabling strategic projects to be supported through EU4Health (and other Union programmes) and linking that funding to supply chain vulnerability assessments, which aligns with the org's call to tie financial mechanisms to health/supply security criteria.

source_text: J&J is committed to having resilient and agile global supply chains to ensure all patients have access to medical goods. We support the EUs intention to address the long-term industrial challenges related to the supply of vulnerable critical medicines. However, policy measures must be fit for purpose, targeting product-specific root causes in line with the EU objectives for simplification & competitiveness. Harmonization & Simplification for EU Competitiveness. The Act should reflect the EU's ambitions for a competitive life sciences ecosystem and consider the cumulative impact of EU initiatives (incl. pharma package, UWWTD, PFAS etc) and similar national initiatives on the competitiveness of the EU pharma industry. It should focus on harmonizing EU/national measures to avoid duplication/additional complexity. Simplification is key to avoiding overburdening supply chains/diverting resources from effectively managing supply chain risk. A proportionate, risk-based approach customized to product-specific root causes (mature vs on-patent medicines) is essential. EU coordination of national incentives is needed to ensure proportionality and avoid internal market disruption. Focus on Vulnerable Critical Medicines to prioritize efforts. Differentiation between critical & vulnerable critical medicines is essential. Harmonization and co-ordination of definitions/methodologies across EU & national legislation is key to avoiding complexity & inefficiency in managing shortages
context_after: Harmonization and co-ordination of definitions/methodologies across EU & national legislation is key to avoiding complexity & inefficiency in managing shortages. They should only account for approved, reimbursed and marketed products and indications and potential shortcomings in addressing local patient needs (instead of local demand). Existing MAH risk management processes that are designed to secure supply continuity and minimize shortages and include capacity back-ups should be considered when assessing vulnerability. Global manufacturing is crucial for resilient supply chains and production outside of the EU should not be automatically considered a higher risk/vulnerability. Harmonization of Contingency Stock requirements to improve flexibility. Uncoordinated national stockpiling requirements are counterproductive and impede the flexible allocation of stock. EU Solidarity is important to consider as stockpiling can directly impact patients in neighboring countries. Stockpiling should be coordinated at EU level, focusing on vulnerable critical medicines with a risk-based, proportionate and transparent approach to ensure reallocation flexibility and solidarity with other supply stakeholders (wholesalers, pharmacies) to minimize cost/waste. Location flexibility, multi-country/EU packs & ePILs would increase allocation flexibility. EMVS data should be used to monitor supply, demand and stock levels in real time
article_text:
Article 1
Objectives and subject matter 1.The objective of this Regulation is to strengthen the security of supply and the availability of critical medicinal products within the Union, thereby ensuring a high level of public health protection and supporting the security of the Union. The objective of this Regulation is also to improve the availability and accessibility of other medicinal products, where the functioning of the market does not otherwise sufficiently ensure the availability and accessibility of those medicinal products to patients, whilst giving due consideration to the appropriateness to ensure the affordability of medicinal products. 2.To achieve the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Regulation sets out a framework to: (a)facilitate investments in manufacturing capacity for critical medicinal products, their active substances and other key inputs in the Union; (b)lower the risk of supply disruptions and strengthen availability by incentivising supply chain diversification and resilience in the public procurement procedures of critical medicinal products and other medicinal products of common interest; (c)leverage the aggregated demand of participating Member States through collaborative procurement procedures, and (d)support the diversification of supply chains also by facilitating the conclusion of strategic partnerships.
provision_effect: Establishes the objectives and framework of the regulation to strengthen security of supply, availability of critical medicinal products, facilitate manufacturing investments, incentivise supply chain diversification, leverage aggregated demand through collaborative procurement, and support strategic partnerships.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: J&J explicitly supports EU action to address long-term industrial challenges related to supply of critical medicines and advocates for supply chain resilience, diversification, and a proportionate risk-based approach — all of which are directly reflected in the provision's stated objectives and framework (facilitating manufacturing investments, incentivising supply chain diversification, and supporting strategic partnerships). While J&J raises concerns about proportionality and complexity, their core advocacy direction — strengthening supply chain resilience for critical medicinal products — aligns with the concrete actions the provision establishes.

source_text: Ref. A res(2025)1587545 - 27/02/2025 MedTech Europe Response for the EU Commissions call for evidence on a Critical Medicines Act. 27 February 2025 Response MedTech Europe is providing the following feedback on behalf of its members, representing the medical technology sector, which includes diagnostics, medical devices and digital health solutions. Both the pharmaceutical and the medical technology sectors operate in the healthcare space, providing critical medical solutions to healthcare systems and patients across the EU. However, the supply chains, logistics and usage of medical devices are fundamentally different to those of pharmaceuticals. The medical technology sector has deeper supply chains (we have received reports of supply chains up to 30 levels deep) and spanning a complex combination of materials and technologies necessary for the effective development and delivery of medical technologies including diagnostics and digital health solutions. The regulatory environments of both sectors are also fundamentally different. For medical technologies, a specific obligation and process for reporting interruption of supply has already been created under of the Medical Devices Regulations (both under the MDR and the IVDR). In contrast to the pharmaceutical sector, no dedicated evidence collection or stakeholder consultation was conducted for medical technologies
context_after: In contrast to the pharmaceutical sector, no dedicated evidence collection or stakeholder consultation was conducted for medical technologies. Effectively this means that solutions to shortages which are effective for pharmaceuticals are not necessarily going to be effective for medical technologies, which is an important consideration when defining the scope of the Critical Medicines Act. The experience of recent strains on medical technologies supply chains since the COVID-19 pandemic has shown that medical technologies are subject to separate and distinct pressures when it comes to supply chain vulnerabilities. Therefore, solutions to mitigate shortages which are relevant to the pharmaceutical sector will not be applicable or effective for medical technologies. It is therefore the strong recommendation of MedTech Europe that medical technologies – that is medical devices, diagnostics and digital health solutions - remain excluded from the Critical Medicines Act. Using solutions which address shortages in the pharmaceutical sector will not be effective for medical devices. 1

Article 28
Regulation (EU) 2024/795 is amended as follows: (a)in Article 2, (1) point (a), subparagraph (iii) is replaced by the following: ‘(iii)biotechnologies, and any other technologies relevant for manufacturing of critical medicinal products as defined in Critical Medicines Act *; _________ *Regulation (EU) … of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down a framework for strengthening the availability and security of supply of critical medicinal products as well as for improving the availability of, and access to, medicinal products of common interest, and amending Regulation (EU) 2024/795.’ [D.G.: reference to be completed with the definitive title of the ‘Critical Medicines Act’ and with its publications references once they are available];’ (b)in Article 2, the following subparagraph is added in paragraph 3: ‘By way of derogation from the first subparagraph of this paragraph, the value chain for the development or manufacturing of medicinal products that fall within the scope of the [Critical Medicines Act] and that are referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii) of this Article, relates to finished dosage forms, as well as to active pharmaceutical ingredients and other key inputs necessary for the production of the finished dosage forms of critical medicinal products as defined in the Regulation.’; (c)in article 2, paragraph 8 is added: ‘8.Strategic projects designated in accordance with the [Critical Medicines Act] that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products shall be deemed to contribute to the STEP objective referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii).’; (d)in Article 4, paragraph 7 is replaced by the following: ‘7.Strategic projects recognised in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act [and the Critical Medicines Act] that fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation and that receive a contribution under the programmes referred to in Article 3 of this Regulation may also receive a contribution from any other Union programme, including funds under shared management, provided that those contributions do not cover the same costs. The rules of the relevant Union programme shall apply to the corresponding contribution to the strategic project. The cumulative funding shall not exceed the total eligible costs of the strategic project. The support from the different Union programmes may be calculated on a pro rata basis in accordance with the documents setting out the conditions for support.’; (e)in Article 6, paragraph 1, point c is replaced by the following: (c)details of projects that have been recognized as strategic projects under the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act and the [Critical Medicines Act], to the extent that they fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation. Chapter VIII Final provisions
provision_effect: Amends Regulation (EU) 2024/795 (STEP) to integrate critical medicinal products (as defined in the Critical Medicines Act) into the STEP framework, including value chain definitions, strategic project designations, and cumulative funding eligibility.
label: NOISE
reasoning: MedTech Europe's position focuses on distinguishing the medical technology sector from pharmaceuticals and arguing that the Critical Medicines Act should not conflate the two sectors, but it does not express any specific advocacy stance on the STEP regulation amendments or the integration of critical medicinal products into the STEP framework. The org text is essentially background context and a complaint about lack of consultation, with no directional position on the provision's subject matter.

article_text:
Article 29
Obligation of the market actors to provide information 1.Marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators in the supply and distribution chains of critical medicinal products including their key inputs and active substances or medicinal products of common interest shall upon request provide the Commission or national authorities, as relevant, the requested information necessary for the purpose of application of this Regulation. 2.The Commission and national authorities of the Member States shall aim to avoid duplication of the information requested and submitted. 3.The Commission and national authorities of the Member States shall assess the merits of duly substantiated confidentiality claims made by marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators, requested to provide information per paragraph 1, and shall protect any information that is commercially confidential against unjustified disclosure.
provision_effect: Requires marketing authorisation holders and other economic operators in the supply and distribution chains of critical medicinal products to provide information upon request to the Commission or national authorities, with protections for commercially confidential information and efforts to avoid duplication.
label: NOISE

It does not express a specific directional stance on information-sharing obligations for supply chain actors — it neither advocates for nor against such a mechanism. The text is largely background/contextual, making it NOISE relative to this provision.

source_text: The Critical Medicines Act should therefore take into account the entire supply chain of critical medicine production including chemical raw materials, excipients, as well as packaging materials, which are essential for the safe storage and distribution of medicinal products. The inclusion of the chemical sector, which is responsible for the production of these raw materials, substances and components, is thus crucial to ensure the continuity of pharmaceutical manufacturing operations. 3 Opinion of the European Economic and Social Committee on 'Securing Europe's supply of medicines: the development of an act. "Safeguarding Europe's supply of medicines: Preparing an act on critical medicinal products" CCMI/212
context_before: production of medicines in EU Member States and to support the European production capacity throughout the entire supply chain.3 Meanwhile, current discussions mainly focus on active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and finished medicinal products, neglecting other elements that play an important role in the stability, efficacy and safety of medicines. The stage of the critical medicines production which is most dependent on external suppliers is the sourcing of chemical raw materials, such as pharmaceutical precursors, necessary for the synthesis of active ingredients. The vast majority of these raw materials come from Asia, making Europe rely heavily on imports. Logistical problems, geopolitical disruptions or increased transport costs can lead to severe API shortages, which in turn will affect the availability of medicines across the European Union. Also overlooked in the pharmaceutical safety debate are excipients, which play a key role in the stability, bioavailability and safety of medicines. Modern pharmaceuticals cannot do without preservatives, stabilisers, emulsifiers or lipid carriers, which are essential to ensuring the efficacy of therapies. The lack of strategic regulation in this area and the reliance on a small number of suppliers make excipients undoubtedly a bottleneck in medicines production, as was evident during the COVID- 19 pandemic, when global shortages of lipid carriers delayed the production of mRNA vaccines

[6] Establishing legal grounds for awarding strategic project status to critical medicines projects and preferences for such projects Given the critical importance of ensuring the medicine safety of the EU population, projects that are essential to reducing shortages of products from the EU list of critical medicines should be given strategic project status. Such solutions have already been implemented for projects relevant to the zero-emission sector under the Net-Zero Industry Act and for projects of key significance to the management of critical raw materials under the Critical Raw Materials Act. The strategic status should be granted to projects based on the fulfilment of EU-wide uniform, clearly defined requirements in terms of project size and expected results. Strategic projects approved by a competent Member State authority should be prioritised by significantly reducing times required to grant them all necessary administrative permits or by ensuring preferential access to raw materials. In terms of funding, having a strategic status should entitle to preferences under relevant programmes and funding sources at national and EU level (e.g. by awarding additional points). It is also extremely important to develop solutions facilitating assembly of funding from different public sources (cumulation of public aid), as discussed in more detail in section 8 below

article_text:
Article 7
Priority status of strategic projects Strategic projects shall be considered as contributing to the security of supply of critical medicinal products in the Union and, therefore, to be in the public interest. The Member States’ authorities shall ensure that the relevant permit granting processes related to strategic projects are carried out in the fastest way possible, making available, in particular, any form of accelerated procedures that exists in applicable Union and national law.
provision_effect: Grants strategic projects priority status as contributing to security of supply of critical medicinal products, requiring Member States to carry out permit-granting processes as fast as possible, including through accelerated procedures.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocated for establishing legal grounds for strategic project status for critical medicines projects, with preferences including significantly reduced times for administrative permits — precisely what Article 7 delivers by granting priority status and mandating accelerated permit-granting procedures. The provision directly responds to the org's specific ask on both the strategic status mechanism and the administrative permit acceleration.

source_text: [5] Increase in state aid for R&D activities related to the manufacturing of critical medicines As the EESC emphasises, 'To strengthen the European pharmaceutical industry, we need to (...) stimulate research and development efforts (...) and create financial and institutional support in order to restore the production of active substances and essential medicines, i.e. generics, in the EU '. Indeed, bringing pharmaceutical production back to Europe on a larger scale will entail a number of accompanying investment projects in research and development, including the optimisation of the manufacturing technologies. Inspired by the provisions of other EU regulations, in particular including the Chip Act2 , it also seems reasonable to increase the preference for the implementation of the R&D components of projects related to critical medicines manufacturing. Under the framework for state aid for research, development and innovation activities, maximum allowed aid intensity could be up to 80% for aid to R&D projects of medium-sized enterprises and up to 90% for projects of small enterprises. Thus, state aid for research and development activities related to the production of critical medicines could be up to 70% of the project value for large enterprises, 80% for medium-sized enterprises and 90% for small enterprises
context_before: Due to the level of economic development, more and more EU regions are losing the privilege of benefitting from regional investment aid, which is intended to support less developed regions. This leads to a situation where companies based and operating in certain areas are deprived of investment support (virtually of any kind, including tax exemptions). In the case of Poland, this applies in particular to the Warsaw Capital Region (in which such manufacturers of medicines as Polfa Tarchomin, Polpharma Biologics, Biomed Serum and Vaccine Manufacturing, Adamed and Bioton are located). Without the changes referred to in 3 above, these enterprises are deprived of the possibility to benefit from any public support for their investments, which are in many cases strategic from the point of view of Poland and the EU, in the area of medicine safety. Large companies from the Wielkopolskie Province (Biofarm, Farmapol, GlaxoSmithKline) and the Dolnośląskie Voivodeship (US Pharmacia or Hasco-Lek) also face significant restrictions. Of course, this issue is not specific to Poland only: similar challenges are also faced by companies in Hungary (Budapest Capital Region - Gedeon Richter, Egis Pharmaceuticals, Sanofi or Teva, among others), the Czech Republic (Prague Capital Region - Zentiva), Slovenia (mainly Ljubljana Region - Lek) or Romania (Bucharest Capital Region - Antibotice, Biofarm, Terapia or Zentiva)

Regulatory and scientific support from medicines agencies and pharmaceutical inspectorates 1.Upon request of a project promoter, a Member State shall provide regulatory support to a strategic project located on its territory, including by prioritising Good Manufacturing Practices inspections for approval of new and extended manufacturing sites and for the manufacturing sites modernised in the context of the concerned strategic project. 2.Upon request of a project promoter, the European Medicines Agency (‘the Agency’) shall provide dedicated advice to assist project promoters developing projects relying on innovative manufacturing processes.

Strategic projects approved by a competent Member State authority should be prioritised by significantly reducing times required to grant them all necessary administrative permits or by ensuring preferential access to raw materials. In terms of funding, having a strategic status should entitle to preferences under relevant programmes and funding sources at national and EU level (e.g. by awarding additional points). It is also extremely important to develop solutions facilitating assembly of funding from different public sources (cumulation of public aid), as discussed in more detail in section 8 below

article_text:
Article 15
Financial support by Member States 1.Without prejudice to Articles 107 and 108 TFEU, Member States may prioritise financial support to strategic projects that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products identified following a vulnerability evaluation and with due consideration to the strategic orientations of the Critical Medicines Group referred to in Article 26(2) point (a). 2.For as long as the critical medicinal product is on the Union List of Critical Medicinal Products, an undertaking that has benefitted from financial support for a strategic project shall prioritise supply to the Union market and use its very best efforts to ensure that the critical medicinal product remains available in the Member States where it is being marketed. 3.The Member State that provided financial support to a strategic project may request such undertaking to provide the necessary supplies of a critical medicinal product, active substance or key inputs, as applicable, to the Union market to avoid shortages in one or several Member States. Any Member State that encounters a threat of shortages of the critical medicinal product in question may demand the Member State that provided financial support to submit a request on its behalf.
provision_effect: Allows Member States to prioritise financial support to strategic projects addressing vulnerabilities in critical medicinal product supply chains, and requires beneficiaries to prioritise supply to the Union market and respond to shortage requests.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for establishing legal grounds for awarding strategic project status to critical medicines projects and for those projects to receive preferential access to funding at national and EU level. Article 15 directly delivers on this by enabling Member States to prioritise financial support to strategic projects addressing supply chain vulnerabilities in critical medicines, which is precisely the mechanism the organisation called for — mirroring analogous frameworks under the Net-Zero Industry Act and Critical Raw Materials Act that the organisation itself cited as models.

source_text: It is therefore necessary to put in place mechanisms that will effectively encourage the launch and increase of the production of critical medicines and their ingredients in Europe. Financial dimension [1] It seems absolutely crucial to create at EU level an "API and Essential Medicines Fund" managed by a central EU institution (section 1) operating with the support and financial commitment of Member States, the European Investment Bank, as well as private partners (banks, investment funds, etc.) to finance strategic investments in infrastructure for the production of medicines, active substances and critical ingredients. Individual Member States should not compete with each other for strategic investment - the role of the EC and the bodies responsible for implementing the CMA should be to diversify the geographical distribution of pharmaceutical production appropriately, fairly and in line with the capacities and competences of individual Member States. As the European Economic and Social Committee (EESC)1 highlights in its position paper, 'The production of active substances and population medicines supported by European funds should be subject to geographical diversification and diversification in relation to external threats, 1 European Economic and Social Committee 'Securing Europe’s medicine supply: envisioning a Critical Medicines Act CCMI/212
context_before: Ref. Ares(2025)1458218 - 24/02/2025 Draft position of Medicines for Poland on the key provisions of the Critical Medicines Act (CMA) Europe's growing dependence on the supply of raw materials necessary for pharmaceutical production from sources outside our continent has been the subject of debate at EU level for several years. The Critical Medicines Act, which has been announced for years and explicitly mentioned in the 'EU Competitiveness Compass' published at the end of January, aims to increase their production in Europe and reduce reliance on non-EU supplies. In order to be able to achieve these goals, a well-considered, holistic regulatory and financial framework is needed to bring pharmaceutical production back to Europe and to become independent from Asian suppliers. In recent years, the EU pharmaceutical industry has experienced a decline in competitiveness and relocation of production, jobs, clinical trials and R&D operations to Asia and the US. Over the last two decades, the investment gap between the US and the EU has grown from EUR 2 billion to a staggering EUR 25 billion, representing a thousand per cent increase in the disparity. Meanwhile, the EU remains the second largest pharmaceutical market in the world. It is therefore necessary to put in place mechanisms that will effectively encourage the launch and increase of the production of critical medicines and their ingredients in Europe

Compensating for the difference in production costs between the EU and Asia is a necessary expense that warrants the enhanced security of EU citizens and that must be financed by new, dedicated EU funds. Article 27
Strategic partnerships Without prejudice to the prerogatives of the Council, the Commission, shall explore possibilities of concluding strategic partnerships aiming to diversify sourcing of critical medicinal products, their active substances and key inputs to increase the security of supply of critical medicinal products in the Union. Article 28
Regulation (EU) 2024/795 is amended as follows: (a)in Article 2, (1) point (a), subparagraph (iii) is replaced by the following: ‘(iii)biotechnologies, and any other technologies relevant for manufacturing of critical medicinal products as defined in Critical Medicines Act *; _________ *Regulation (EU) … of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down a framework for strengthening the availability and security of supply of critical medicinal products as well as for improving the availability of, and access to, medicinal products of common interest, and amending Regulation (EU) 2024/795.’ [D.G.: reference to be completed with the definitive title of the ‘Critical Medicines Act’ and with its publications references once they are available];’ (b)in Article 2, the following subparagraph is added in paragraph 3: ‘By way of derogation from the first subparagraph of this paragraph, the value chain for the development or manufacturing of medicinal products that fall within the scope of the [Critical Medicines Act] and that are referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii) of this Article, relates to finished dosage forms, as well as to active pharmaceutical ingredients and other key inputs necessary for the production of the finished dosage forms of critical medicinal products as defined in the Regulation.’; (c)in article 2, paragraph 8 is added: ‘8.Strategic projects designated in accordance with the [Critical Medicines Act] that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products shall be deemed to contribute to the STEP objective referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii).’; (d)in Article 4, paragraph 7 is replaced by the following: ‘7.Strategic projects recognised in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act [and the Critical Medicines Act] that fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation and that receive a contribution under the programmes referred to in Article 3 of this Regulation may also receive a contribution from any other Union programme, including funds under shared management, provided that those contributions do not cover the same costs. The rules of the relevant Union programme shall apply to the corresponding contribution to the strategic project. The cumulative funding shall not exceed the total eligible costs of the strategic project. The support from the different Union programmes may be calculated on a pro rata basis in accordance with the documents setting out the conditions for support.’; (e)in Article 6, paragraph 1, point c is replaced by the following: (c)details of projects that have been recognized as strategic projects under the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act and the [Critical Medicines Act], to the extent that they fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation. Chapter VIII Final provisions
provision_effect: Amends Regulation (EU) 2024/795 (STEP) to integrate the Critical Medicines Act by designating strategic projects under that Act as contributing to STEP objectives, enabling cumulation of EU funding for such projects, and requiring reporting on recognised strategic projects — effectively granting strategic project status and funding preferences to critical medicines projects.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocated for establishing legal grounds for awarding strategic project status to critical medicines projects and for facilitating cumulation of public funding from different sources, drawing a direct parallel to the Net-Zero Industry Act and Critical Raw Materials Act. The provision does exactly this: it amends STEP to recognise Critical Medicines Act strategic projects on par with those under the Net-Zero Industry Act and Critical Raw Materials Act, allows cumulation of EU funding, and integrates critical medicines into STEP objectives — directly delivering what the organisation asked for.
