article_text:
Article 22
Commission procurement on behalf of or in the name of Member States 1.By way of derogation from Article 168(3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509 where nine or more Member States jointly request the Commission to procure on their behalf, or in their name, the Commission may initiate a procurement procedure under the conditions set out in this Article when the procurement relates to medicinal products belonging to one of the following categories below; (a)critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability evaluation has identified a vulnerability in the supply chains or for which the MSSG has recommended a common procurement initiative; (b)medicinal products of common interest, for which a joint clinical assessment report has been published pursuant to Article 12(4) Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 of the European Parliament and the Council , or which have undergone a clinical assessment carried out under the voluntary cooperation among Member States as per Article 23(1) point (e) of that Regulation. 2.The joint request referred to in paragraph 1 shall only be made where the medicinal product concerned fulfils one of the criteria set out in that paragraph and if the requested procurement procedure will help to improve the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the Union or ensure the availability and accessibility of medicinal products of common interest, as applicable. 3.The participation in the procurement procedure shall be open to all Member States. The Commission shall inform all Member States of the request, through the Critical Medicines Group, and invite them to join the procedure. 4.The Commission shall assess the utility, necessity and proportionality of the request and whether the request is justified in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall in particular verify whether the procurement could constitute discrimination or restriction to trade or a distortion to competition. 5.The Commission shall inform the interested Member States within one month of the request of its decision and state its reasons in case of a refusal. 6.If in light of the Commission assessment, it is necessary, in order to achieve the objectives of this Regulation, to conduct the procurement as exclusive for the Member States or to agree to minimum binding quantities, the Commission agreement to pursue the procedure may be conditioned upon acceptance of these conditions by interested Member States. 7.Except for the derogations provided for in this Regulation, the procurement referred to in this Article shall be carried out in accordance with Article 168 (3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing the Commission to conduct joint procurement of critical or common-interest medicinal products on behalf of nine or more requesting Member States, subject to conditions of utility, necessity, proportionality, and non-distortion of competition.
label: NOISE
reasoning: UNESSA's position consists of high-level endorsements of a European approach to critical medicines, general calls for impact assessments, and broad concerns about financial burdens — none of which constitute a specific directional stance on joint Commission procurement mechanisms. There is no specific advocacy position on whether such a procurement mechanism should exist, how it should be structured, or what conditions should govern it.

source_text: 27 February 2025 effectively. Hospitals, pharmacies and other players in the sector must be able to focus on operational and clinical issues without being encumbered by excessive bureaucracy. The measures to be taken in this context should also take account of existing practices. A relevant example can be found in the field of public procurement: the strategic report of the Critical Medicines Alliance advices to add MEAT criteria, but the current legal framework relating to public procurement already allows purchases to be made using criteria other than price alone, in particular quality, supply chain (with penalties for non-delivery) and sustainability criteria (as far as possible). In practice To ensure the successful implementation of the future regulation, UNESSA would also like to draw attention to the importance of clear and well thought-out procedures from the legislation stage onwards, in collaboration with all stakeholders. Several aspects need to be clarified to ensure effective operationalisation: 1. Stock policy: One of the major challenges lies in the management of stocks shared between and within countries. It is essential to define how, when and by whom these stocks would be accessed in the event of a shortage in a member country. The procedure must be rapid and well co-ordinated to respond to emergencies, with clearly defined deadlines. 2
context_before: In addition, a better pooling of efforts at European level would be beneficial for rapidly identifying alternatives in the event of a shortage. During past shortages, for example, cooperation between Member States to share stocks of certain life-saving medicines helped to ensure continuity of care. In the event of a shortage of specific treatments, such coordination could make it possible to quickly redirect available stocks to the regions or countries most affected. Setting up sharing and coordination mechanisms on this scale would thus ensure greater responsiveness and greater resilience in the European healthcare system to the challenges of managing critical medicines. What impact will this have on the ground? Over and above these implications and observations, there are a number of impacts on the ground that we feel are essential to monitor. Limiting the administrative burden UNESSA stresses the importance, in the future Critical Medicines Act, of ensuring that the administrative burden remains limited and proportionate. For the system to be effective, the procedures for calling up European stocks must be simple. It is vital not to add an unnecessary layer of legislation that would add to the administrative burden without providing any real added value. Simplifying processes and reducing administrative complexity will be crucial to ensuring that the new regulations are implemented 3
context_after: The procedure must be rapid and well co-ordinated to respond to emergencies, with clearly defined deadlines. 2. Stocks database: A centralised database on stocks of critical medicines should be set up to ensure effective transparency and coordination between Member States. However, it remains to be defined who will be responsible for managing this database, as well as the access rules for the various players. It is essential for this information to be available in a fluid manner and in real time to all players in the distribution and management chain. 3. Multi-country packaging and labelling: Finally, the logistical impact of packaging and labelling medicines for several countries must be taken into account. It is important to clarify what changes are envisaged in this area and to measure their impact on medicines management structures. The introduction of multilingual labels complying with the requirements of each country could lead to additional costs and delays for manufacturers, as well as logistical repercussions for distributors and hospitals. Support measures to accompany these adaptations will probably have to be considered. 4. Distribution: the link between "ordinary" public procurement contracts and strategic stocks should be clearly established for reasons of stock rotation

Agreement concerning procedures under Articles 22 and 23 1.Member States participating in the procurement procedures covered by Articles 22 and 23 shall share with the Commission any information relevant for the procurement procedure. Member States shall provide resources necessary for the successful conclusion of the procedure, in particular through involvement of staff with expertise and knowledge. 2.An agreement between the Member States and the Commission shall determine the practical arrangements governing the procurement procedure, liabilities to be assumed and the decision-making process. article_text:
Article 26
Tasks of the Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.The Critical Medicines Group shall facilitate coordination in the implementation of this Regulation and, where appropriate, advise the Commission, so as to maximise the impact of the measures envisaged and to avoid any unintended effects on the internal market. 2.In order to attain the objectives referred to in paragraph 1, the Critical Medicines Group shall perform the following tasks: (a)facilitate coordination on strategic orientation of the financial support for strategic projects, including by exchanging information on the manufacturing capacity for a given critical medicinal product, existing or planned, in the Member States and facilitate discussion on the capacity needed in the Union to strengthen its supply security and availability of critical medicinal products within the Union; (b)facilitate exchanges on the national programmes referred to in Article 19 and enable cooperation on and coordination of Member States public procurement policies with regard to critical medicinal products; (c)facilitate discussion of the need for a collaborative procurement initiative for a given medicinal product; (d)advise the MSSG to provide the order of priority of critical medicinal products for vulnerability evaluation, and propose a review or an update of existing evaluations where necessary. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall enable the exchanges of information between the Member States and the Commission as referred to in Article 17 and shall enable, where necessary, a coordination of respective actions aiming to attain the objectives of this Regulation. 4.The Critical Medicines Group shall periodically discuss the potential contribution of strategic partnerships to the objectives of this Regulation, prioritisation of third countries for this purpose, and the consistency and potential synergies between Member States’ cooperation with relevant third countries and the actions carried out by the Union. 5.The Critical Medicines Group, at the Commission’s request, may provide an opinion on matters related to the application of this Regulation in the context of performing tasks as referred to in this Article. Chapter VI International cooperation
provision_effect: Establishes the Critical Medicines Coordination Group's tasks, including facilitating coordination on strategic orientation, financial support for strategic projects, national programmes, procurement policies, collaborative procurement, vulnerability evaluations, and international partnerships.

Article 28
Regulation (EU) 2024/795 is amended as follows: (a)in Article 2, (1) point (a), subparagraph (iii) is replaced by the following: ‘(iii)biotechnologies, and any other technologies relevant for manufacturing of critical medicinal products as defined in Critical Medicines Act *; (c)in article 2, paragraph 8 is added: ‘8.Strategic projects designated in accordance with the [Critical Medicines Act] that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products shall be deemed to contribute to the STEP objective referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii).’; The rules of the relevant Union programme shall apply to the corresponding contribution to the strategic project. The support from the different Union programmes may be calculated on a pro rata basis in accordance with the documents setting out the conditions for support.’; (e)in Article 6, paragraph 1, point c is replaced by the following: (c)details of projects that have been recognized as strategic projects under the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act and the [Critical Medicines Act], to the extent that they fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation. source_text: 3. iAE general position1 A) Buy European. Foster the reshoring of value chains into the EU and foster domestic production. The revision of the public procurement directives should enable preference to be given to European products in public procurement for certain strategic sectors such as pharma. The reshoring of value chain should also include API’s, generics and other specialty chemicals. This could be done through: o Tender specifications that link the public health spending on medicines to a certain degree of domestic production. o When stockpiling for emergencies, conduct purchases from companies with domestic production. However, it must be avoided that stockpiling exacerbates the problem of shortages by taking available medicines off the market. o Tie research funding to a compulsory share of domestic production of the marketed pharmaceutical/active ingredient. B) Intra-EU Fairness Any measure taken by the European authorities must take into consideration the different sizes of markets for medicines within the EU, and therefore different market powers of Member States. Joint public procurement is only one tool to increase equal access to critical medicines. The Act must provide guarantees for fair distribution of medicines across the Union. - Language barriers: The availability problems of critical medicines are worse in small language areas, which often also have smaller population bases

article_text:
Article 18
Incentivising resilience, sustainability and positive social impacts in public procurement procedures 1.For award procedures of critical medicinal products falling within the scope of Directive 2014/24/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council, contracting authorities in the Member States shall apply procurement requirements other than price-only award criteria such as procurement requirements that promote the resilience of supply in the Union. Those procurement requirements shall be defined in accordance with Directive 2014/24/EU and may relate to stockholding obligations, the number of diversified suppliers, monitoring of supply chains, their transparency to the contracting authority and contract performance clauses on timely delivery. 2.With regard to critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability in the supply chains has been confirmed through a vulnerability evaluation pointing to the high level of dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries, the contracting authorities shall, where justified, apply procurement requirements that favour suppliers that manufacture a significant proportion of these critical medicinal products in the Union. These requirements shall be applied in compliance with the Union’s international commitments. 3.With regard to other medicinal products of common interest, where justified by market analysis and public health considerations, the contracting authorities may apply procurement requirements that favour suppliers that manufacture at least a significant proportion of these medicinal products in the Union. These requirements shall be applied in compliance with the Union’s international commitments. 4.This Article shall not preclude contracting authorities from using additional qualitative requirements, including in relation to environmental sustainability and social rights. 5.Contracting authorities may exceptionally decide not to apply paragraphs 1, 2 and 3 where justified by market analysis or considerations related to the financing of health services.
provision_effect: Requires contracting authorities to apply procurement criteria beyond price alone for critical medicinal products, including requirements that favour suppliers manufacturing in the Union, and allows additional qualitative requirements related to sustainability and social rights.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for "Buy European" procurement rules that give preference to European products in pharma public procurement, including tender specifications linking health spending to domestic production — which is precisely what Article 18 establishes by mandating non-price procurement criteria and favouring suppliers that manufacture a significant proportion of critical medicinal products in the Union. The provision directly delivers the specific mechanism the organisation called for.

source_text: 3. iAE general position1 A) Buy European. Foster the reshoring of value chains into the EU and foster domestic production. The revision of the public procurement directives should enable preference to be given to European products in public procurement for certain strategic sectors such as pharma. The reshoring of value chain should also include API’s, generics and other specialty chemicals. This could be done through: o Tender specifications that link the public health spending on medicines to a certain degree of domestic production. o When stockpiling for emergencies, conduct purchases from companies with domestic production. However, it must be avoided that stockpiling exacerbates the problem of shortages by taking available medicines off the market. o Tie research funding to a compulsory share of domestic production of the marketed pharmaceutical/active ingredient. B) Intra-EU Fairness Any measure taken by the European authorities must take into consideration the different sizes of markets for medicines within the EU, and therefore different market powers of Member States. Joint public procurement is only one tool to increase equal access to critical medicines. The Act must provide guarantees for fair distribution of medicines across the Union. - Language barriers: The availability problems of critical medicines are worse in small language areas, which often also have smaller population bases

article_text:
Article 21
Commission facilitated Member States’ cross-border procurement 1.Upon a reasoned request of three or more Member States (‘the request’), the Commission may act as facilitator for the requesting Member States’ cross-border procurement as laid down in Article 39 of Directive of the European Parliament and of the Council 2014/24/EC for medicinal products of common interest. 2.Having received the request, the Commission shall inform all other Member States of the initiative and set an appropriate deadline for them to declare interest. Such a deadline shall not exceed three weeks. 3.The Commission shall assess the request in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall communicate to the interested Member States its decision on whether it agrees, or not, to facilitate the proposed initiative within three weeks of receiving the request. 4.If the Commission declines the request, it shall provide reasons for the refusal. 5.If the Commission accepts the request, the Commission shall provide secretarial and logistical support to the interested Member States. The Commission shall facilitate communication and cooperation between the involved Member States and provide advice on applicable Union public procurement rules and on regulatory matters related to medicinal products. 6.The facilitation offered by the Commission shall be limited in time and end at the latest upon signature of the procurement contract by the participating contracting authorities. 7.The Commission shall not be responsible, nor held liable, for any breaches of Union or national procurement laws by the participating contracting authorities. The Commission shall not bear any liability associated with the conduct of the procurement procedure by interested Member States and implementation of the contract resulting from the procedure.
provision_effect: Establishes a Commission-facilitated cross-border joint procurement mechanism for medicinal products of common interest, where the Commission provides secretarial, logistical, and advisory support to Member States upon request.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for joint public procurement as a tool to increase equal access to critical medicines and calls for measures that account for different market sizes across Member States. The provision directly establishes a Commission-facilitated cross-border procurement mechanism for medicinal products, which aligns with the org's call for joint procurement and intra-EU fairness considerations (e.g., smaller Member States can join larger initiatives via the open invitation to all Member States).

Priority for these partnerships should be given to those countries where adequate environmental criteria and labour criteria is established. - International Solidarity: The European Union defines itself as a community founded on values of solidarity. In order to respect these values of solidarity, the EU must not just supply medicines to its own population but also ensure that the citizens of financially fragile countries also have access to quality medicines. industriAll Europe encourages the creation, development and support of research and production capacities in all regions and across all continents. 4. Conclusion We call for the next Critical Medicines Act to be a real lever for good industrial jobs in Europe and a strong, resilient European pharmaceutical industry. ----------------------------------- IndustriAll European Trade Union represents the voice of 7 million working men and women across supply chains in manufacturing, mining and energy sectors across Europe. article_text:
Article 22
Commission procurement on behalf of or in the name of Member States 1.By way of derogation from Article 168(3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509 where nine or more Member States jointly request the Commission to procure on their behalf, or in their name, the Commission may initiate a procurement procedure under the conditions set out in this Article when the procurement relates to medicinal products belonging to one of the following categories below; (a)critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability evaluation has identified a vulnerability in the supply chains or for which the MSSG has recommended a common procurement initiative; (b)medicinal products of common interest, for which a joint clinical assessment report has been published pursuant to Article 12(4) Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 of the European Parliament and the Council , or which have undergone a clinical assessment carried out under the voluntary cooperation among Member States as per Article 23(1) point (e) of that Regulation. 2.The joint request referred to in paragraph 1 shall only be made where the medicinal product concerned fulfils one of the criteria set out in that paragraph and if the requested procurement procedure will help to improve the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the Union or ensure the availability and accessibility of medicinal products of common interest, as applicable. 3.The participation in the procurement procedure shall be open to all Member States. The Commission shall inform all Member States of the request, through the Critical Medicines Group, and invite them to join the procedure. 4.The Commission shall assess the utility, necessity and proportionality of the request and whether the request is justified in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall in particular verify whether the procurement could constitute discrimination or restriction to trade or a distortion to competition. 5.The Commission shall inform the interested Member States within one month of the request of its decision and state its reasons in case of a refusal. 6.If in light of the Commission assessment, it is necessary, in order to achieve the objectives of this Regulation, to conduct the procurement as exclusive for the Member States or to agree to minimum binding quantities, the Commission agreement to pursue the procedure may be conditioned upon acceptance of these conditions by interested Member States. 7.Except for the derogations provided for in this Regulation, the procurement referred to in this Article shall be carried out in accordance with Article 168 (3) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to conduct joint procurement of critical or common-interest medicinal products on behalf of nine or more Member States, subject to conditions of utility, necessity, proportionality, and non-discrimination.
label: NOISE

The org text contains no specific advocacy stance on Commission-led procurement procedures, their conditions, or their scope.

article_text:
Article 23
Joint Procurement 1.Under conditions laid down in this Article and by way of derogation from Article 168(2) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509, if a contract is necessary for the implementation of the joint action between the Commission and Member States, the Commission and at least nine Member States may engage, as contracting parties, in a joint procurement procedure. 2.A joint procurement procedure may be organised following a request by the Member States or at the Commission’s initiative when the procurement relates to medicinal products belonging to one of the categories below: (a)critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability evaluation has identified a vulnerability in the supply chains or for which the MSSG has recommended a common procurement initiative; (b)medicinal products of common interest, for which a joint clinical assessment report has been published pursuant to Article 12(4) Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 of the European Parliament and the Council , or which have undergone a clinical assessment carried out under the voluntary cooperation among Member States as per Article 23(1) point (e) of that Regulation. 3.The Commission may decide to conduct the joint procurement procedure if the procurement procedure helps to improve the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the Union or ensure the availability and accessibility of medicinal products of common interest, as applicable. 4.The participation in the procurement procedure shall be open to all Member States. The Commission shall inform all Member States of the request through the Critical Medicines Group and invite them to join the procedure. 5.The Commission shall assess the necessity of a joint action and whether the request is justified in light of the objectives of this Regulation. The Commission shall in particular verify whether the procurement could constitute discrimination or restriction to trade or a distortion to competition. 6.If in light of the Commission assessment, it is necessary, in order to achieve the objectives of this Regulation, to conduct the procurement as exclusive for the Member States or to agree to minimum binding quantities, the Commission agreement to pursue the procedure may be conditioned upon acceptance of these conditions by interested Member States. 7.The Commission shall inform the interested Member States within one month of the request of its decision and state its reasons in case of a refusal. 8.Except for the derogations provided for in this Regulation, the joint procurement procedure shall be carried out by the Commission in accordance with Article 168 (2) of Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2024/2509.
provision_effect: Establishes a joint procurement procedure allowing the Commission and at least nine Member States to jointly procure critical medicinal products and medicinal products of common interest, with conditions on participation, exclusivity, and minimum binding quantities.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for joint public procurement as a tool to increase equal access to critical medicines and calls for measures ensuring fair distribution across the Union, including consideration of different Member State market sizes. The provision directly establishes a joint procurement mechanism for critical medicinal products open to all Member States, with the Commission assessing non-discrimination and fair access — aligning with industriAll Europe's specific advocacy for joint procurement and intra-EU fairness in medicine access.
